No `research.json` project is present, so I'll present the guide without persisting. Here is the full locality guide:

---

## 🗺️ Locality Research Guide: Ohio / Northwest Territory, circa 1790

---

### Jurisdictional Context & Boundary Changes

In 1790, **present-day Ohio did not yet exist as a political unit.** The entire region was administered as the **Northwest Territory**, created by the Northwest Ordinance of 1787. This is the single most important jurisdictional fact for your research:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1787–1803 | **Northwest Territory, United States** | Governed from Marietta/Cincinnati; covered modern OH, IN, IL, MI, WI |
| 1803–present | **Ohio, United States** (standardPlace: `Ohio, United States`) | First state carved from the Northwest Territory |

**County formations within the territory were rapid and overlapping:**
- **Washington County (1788)** — the *first* county organized, centered on Marietta (the earliest permanent American settlement in Ohio). Court, deed, and probate records from 1788–1803 for much of Ohio trace here.
- **Hamilton County (1790)** — southwestern Ohio, centered on Cincinnati. Created from the western portion of Washington County.
- Boundary changes were frequent throughout the territorial period. **You must determine which county held jurisdiction at the time and place of the event** — an ancestor living in the same location might appear in Washington County records one year and a newly formed county the next.

**Two competing land-grant systems further divide the territory:**
- 🔵 **Virginia Military District** (between the Scioto and Little Miami Rivers): Used a *metes-and-bounds* survey. Land warrants are at the **Virginia State Library** in Richmond. Deed records are at county courthouses and the Ohio History Connection.
- 🟠 **Connecticut Western Reserve** (northeastern Ohio): Surveyed on a separate township grid. Records are at the **Western Reserve Historical Society** in Cleveland, not at the Ohio History Connection.
- Remaining areas were surveyed under the federal rectangular grid (GLO system).

> **Research trail rule:** If your ancestor's records shift from "Northwest Territory" labeling to "Ohio" or a specific county label after 1803, treat these as a **single continuous trail** — the place did not move, only the jurisdiction name changed.

---

### Available Records & Access Levels

#### 1. Land Records ✅ Best source for this period

Land records are the **primary genealogical source** for pre-statehood Ohio. Settlers had to establish legal title, making these the most consistently documented events of the era.

**FamilySearch collections overlapping 1787–1810:**
- **Ohio, Land Records, 1787–1840** (Collection [1542187](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542187)) — 234,567 records, 117,283 images. *Access: indexed.*
- **Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records, 1788–1799** (Image Group 005112233) — 51 digitized images. **⚠️ Browse-only: 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be browsed image by image.

**Physical/external repositories:**
- **BLM General Land Office (GLO) website** (glorecords.blm.gov): Federal land patents for the public-land states including Ohio.
- **Virginia State Library** (Richmond): Virginia Military District warrants.
- **Western Reserve Historical Society** (Cleveland): Connecticut Western Reserve patents and deeds.
- **Ohio History Connection** (Columbus): Early court and land records for the territorial and early statehood period.

#### 2. Tax Records ⚠️ Partial coverage

Tax lists are a substitute for census records and are particularly valuable in this period because **the 1790 federal census did not cover the Northwest Territory** (Ohio was not yet a state and was not enumerated).

- **Ohio, Tax Records, 1800–1850** (Collection [1987542](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987542)) — 345,890 records. Starts at 1800; does not cover 1787–1799. *Access: indexed.*
- **Washington County, Ohio Tax Records, 1799–1803** (Image Group 005112240) — 73 digitized images, **12% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Partially indexed; remainder must be browsed.
- Pre-1800 tax records, if they survive, exist primarily at the **Ohio History Connection** or county courthouse level and have limited digitization.

#### 3. Church Records ✅ Valuable for vital events

Civil registration of births and deaths did not begin in Ohio until **1867** (statewide) — meaning for 1790, **church records are the only systematic source for baptisms, marriages, and burials.**

- **Ohio, Church Records, 1787–1950** (Collection [2246917](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2246917)) — 678,234 records, 339,117 images. *Access: indexed.* Coverage begins at 1787; check individual record dates.
- Denominations active in the territory by 1790 include Methodist, Presbyterian, and Baptist (brought by settlers from Virginia, Kentucky, and New England). Catholic records exist around French settlements (e.g., near the Great Lakes).
- Denominational archives (e.g., United Methodist Archives, Drew University; Presbyterian Historical Society, Philadelphia) hold records not yet on FamilySearch.

#### 4. Marriage Records ✅ Some coverage

- **Ohio, County Marriages, 1789–2013** (Collection [1803785](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1803785)) — 1,523,678 records, 761,839 images. Coverage begins 1789. *Access: indexed.*
- Earliest entries will be from **Washington County (from 1789)** and **Hamilton County (from 1790)**. Always note which county's register you are consulting.

#### 5. Census Records ⚠️ Major gap; substitutes required

- The **1790 federal census does not include Ohio** — the Northwest Territory was not enumerated.
- The **1800 federal census** is the first to include Ohio, but only the southeastern portion (Washington County area). Coverage is incomplete and some schedules do not survive.
- **No territorial census** is known to have survived for this period.
- **Tax lists (see above) are the primary census substitute for 1787–1800.**

#### 6. Military Records ✅ Particularly relevant

The Northwest Territory was an active military theater in 1790 (the Northwest Indian War, culminating in the Battle of Fallen Timbers, 1794). Military service records are a prime source:

- **Fold3** (fold3.com): Revolutionary War pension files and bounty land warrants — many Ohio settlers were veterans. NARA holdings include service records.
- **NARA** (archives.gov): Pension and bounty-land warrant application files often include depositions naming family members, ages, and places of residence.
- **Ohio Adjutant General records** (Ohio History Connection): State militia records.

#### 7. Probate Records ⚠️ Sparse pre-1803

- Probate was handled at the territorial level before county courts were well-established.
- Early surviving probate files are at the **Ohio History Connection** in Columbus.
- No digitized probate volumes for Ohio appear in FamilySearch's volume_search for this period.

#### 8. Newspapers ⚠️ Very limited

- The *Centinel of the North-Western Territory* (Cincinnati, 1793) is the first newspaper in the region. Very few issues survive.
- **Newspapers.com** and **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) may have limited coverage.

#### 9. Death Records ❌ Out of period

- **Ohio, County Death Records, 1840–2001** — starts in 1840; not applicable to the 1790 target period.

---

### Repository Summary

| Repository | What It Holds |
|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Land records 1787–1840, marriages 1789+, church records 1787+, tax records 1800+ |
| **Ohio History Connection**, Columbus | Territorial-era court, land, and probate records; early county records |
| **BLM GLO Website** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents for all Ohio outside the Virginia Military District |
| **Virginia State Library**, Richmond | Virginia Military District land warrants |
| **Western Reserve Historical Society**, Cleveland | Connecticut Western Reserve land records and local history |
| **NARA** (archives.gov) | Military pensions, bounty land warrants, 1800+ federal census |
| **Fold3** (fold3.com) | Military service records, pension files |
| **County courthouses** | Deed books, probate files (original records still held locally) |

---

### Key Research Quirks & Gotchas

1. **Ohio was not enumerated in the 1790 federal census** — use tax lists and land records as direct substitutes for that decade.
2. **Boundary changes were constant 1788–1803.** Always identify which county held jurisdiction for your ancestor's exact location and date — Washington County records cover most of Ohio before Hamilton County was formed in 1790, and dozens more counties were carved out after statehood.
3. **Northwest Territory records filed under a different place name than Ohio.** FamilySearch's volume_search returns territorial land records under "Northwest Territory, United States" and county-level records under "Washington County, Ohio" or "Hamilton County, Ohio" — search all of these, not just "Ohio."
4. **Civil registration of births and deaths did not begin until 1867.** For vital events before that date, church records are the only systematic source.
5. **Three separate land survey systems** operate in Ohio: the federal rectangular grid, the Virginia Military District (metes-and-bounds), and the Connecticut Western Reserve (separate township grid). Identify which district your ancestor lived in before selecting a repository.
6. **Virginia Military District warrants are NOT in Ohio** — they are at the Virginia State Library in Richmond.
7. **The earliest digitized Northwest Territory land records on FamilySearch (image group 005112233) are browse-only** — 51 images, 0% indexed, not full-text searchable. You must page through them manually.

---

### Digitization Assessment

| Record Type | FamilySearch Access Level |
|---|---|
| Ohio Land Records 1787–1840 | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Ohio Marriages 1789+ | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Ohio Church Records 1787+ | ✅ Indexed + images |
| Ohio Tax Records 1800+ | ✅ Indexed + images |
| NW Territory Land Records 1788–1799 | ⚠️ Browse-only (0% indexed) |
| Washington Co. Tax Records 1799–1803 | ⚠️ Mostly browse-only (12% indexed) |
| Territorial Probate / Court Records | ❌ Not digitized; physical at Ohio History Connection |
| Pre-1800 tax lists | ❌ Digitization gap; check Ohio History Connection |
| Death records | ❌ Out of period (earliest 1840) |

---

### Where to Start

1. **Identify the county** your ancestor would have been in around 1790 (Washington County for most locations; Hamilton County for southwestern Ohio). Use a historical county boundary map — the **Ohio Genealogy Society** and **NewEnglandAncestors.org** publish these.
2. **Search FamilySearch Ohio Land Records 1787–1840** (Collection 1803785) — this is the best-indexed record set touching this period.
3. **Browse the Northwest Territory Territorial Land Records** (image group 005112233) — 51 images, browse-only.
4. **Check BLM GLO** for federal land patents.
5. **Search FamilySearch Ohio County Marriages 1789+** and **Church Records 1787+** for vital events.
6. **For military ancestors:** search NARA pension files and Fold3 — the Northwest Indian War (1785–1795) and Revolutionary War are both relevant.
7. **Contact the Ohio History Connection** for any territorial-era court, probate, or pre-1800 records not yet digitized.

Let me start by establishing the jurisdictional context.Good — `standardPlace` is **"California, United States"** (state from 1850; previously Mexican territory through 1848). Now I'll fire the full survey batch in parallel.I now have all the data needed. Let me compile the full guide.

---

# 📍 California Gold Rush Era — Locality Research Guide
**Place:** California, United States | **Period:** 1848–1865 | **Focus:** Mining migration & settlement

---

## Jurisdictional Context

| Milestone | Date |
|---|---|
| Gold discovered at Sutter's Mill, Coloma | 24 Jan 1848 |
| Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo — California ceded by Mexico to U.S. | Feb 1848 |
| California admitted as 31st state (bypassed territorial status) | 9 Sep 1850 |
| First U.S. federal census taken in California | 1850 |
| California state census | 1852 |

**Why this matters for records:** Because California went straight from Mexican territory to U.S. state, there is no territorial-era U.S. civil record set. The first federal census (1850) is the earliest federal enumeration — and it was *partially destroyed*, with some county returns missing. This makes the **1852 California State Census** uniquely important as a substitute. Records before 1848 were created under Mexican or Spanish authority (see Mission Records below).

---

## Record Sets — Digitized & Online

### 🟢 1. California State Census, 1852
- **FamilySearch volume:** DGS 006601001 — 522 images, covering 1850–1852
- **Access:** **88% name-indexed + full-text searchable** — highly searchable on FamilySearch
- **Why essential:** The 1850 federal census has missing county returns for California; the 1852 state census fills that gap for Gold Rush arrivals. This is often the earliest census record for a 49er.
- **Repository:** FamilySearch (free)

---

### 🟢 2. California, County Births, 1850–1919
- **FamilySearch collection:** [2017276](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2017276) — 789,456 records, 394,728 images
- **Access:** Indexed + images
- **⚠️ Gap warning:** California did *not* require statewide birth registration until 1905. County-level registration began at statehood (1850) but coverage in the 1850s is very sparse — many Gold Rush-era births went entirely unrecorded. Treat absence of a birth record as normal for this period.
- **Repository:** FamilySearch (free)

---

### 🟢 3. California, County Marriages, 1850–1952
- **FamilySearch collection:** [1452832](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1452832) — 567,890 records, 283,945 images
- **Access:** Indexed + images
- **Note:** County marriage licenses begin from statehood (1850). Coverage is better than birth records for this era because marriages required a license.
- **Repository:** FamilySearch (free)

---

### 🟡 4. Mining Claim Registers — San Francisco, 1849–1855
- **FamilySearch volume:** DGS 006601002 — 340 images
- **Access:** ⚠️ **BROWSE-ONLY — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be examined image by image. No search shortcut exists.
- **Content:** County-recorder mining claim records documenting individual claims, often with miner's name, claim location, and date filed.
- **Also:** Original mining claim records for individual counties are held at **county recorders' offices** (e.g., El Dorado, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Placer, Nevada counties — the core Mother Lode belt). Not all have been digitized.
- **Repository:** FamilySearch (browse only); county courthouses for originals

---

### 🟡 5. Port Arrival Registers — Sacramento, 1849–1858
- **FamilySearch volume:** DGS 006601003 — 298 images
- **Access:** ⚠️ **BROWSE-ONLY — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be browsed image by image.
- **Note:** Most '49ers arrived by sea through San Francisco. Ship passenger manifests for **San Francisco arrivals** (the primary port of entry) are held at **NARA's Pacific Region (San Bruno, CA)** and are also available on Ancestry.com. These are distinct from this Sacramento volume and are better indexed.
- **Repositories:** FamilySearch (browse); NARA San Bruno; Ancestry.com

---

### 🟢 6. California, Great Registers, 1866–1910
- **FamilySearch collection:** [1923340](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1923340) — 1,345,678 records, 672,839 images
- **Access:** Indexed + images
- **Date range note:** The Great Registers begin in **1866**, just after the 1850s window — but they are *critical* for Gold Rush research because they capture men who arrived during the Rush and stayed. Each entry typically records name, age, birthplace, occupation, physical description, and county of residence.
- **⚠️ Exclusion:** Only white male citizens were registered. Women, Chinese immigrants, Native Americans, and non-citizens were excluded — so this source systematically underdocuments large portions of the Gold Rush population.
- **Repository:** FamilySearch (free)

---

### 🔵 7. California, Mission Records, 1769–1850
- **FamilySearch collection:** [1804372](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1804372) — 98,765 records, 49,382 images
- **Access:** Indexed + images
- **Period note:** This collection ends at 1850 — it is **pre-Gold Rush background context**, not a prime source for 49ers. Use it for families who were already in California (Californios, Spanish/Mexican settlers, Native Californians) before the rush.
- **Repository:** FamilySearch (free)

---

## Federal & Major External Records

### 8. U.S. Federal Census (1850, 1860)
- **1850:** First federal census in California. **Partially destroyed** — several county schedules are missing. Supplement with the 1852 state census.
- **1860:** Complete and searchable. Captures the settled Gold Rush population.
- **Mortality schedules:** The 1850 and 1860 mortality schedules list deaths in the preceding year — useful for miners who died during the Rush.
- **Access:** Indexed + images on Ancestry.com and FamilySearch

---

### 9. Ship Passenger Manifests — San Francisco
- **Where:** The bulk of sea-route arrivals entered through San Francisco. Manifests are held at **NARA Pacific Region, 1000 Commodore Dr., San Bruno, CA 94066**, and many are digitized on **Ancestry.com**.
- **Routes:** Passengers arrived via Cape Horn, via Panama (overland crossing), and via Nicaragua — all terminating at San Francisco. Knowing the arrival route helps locate the right manifest series.
- **Access:** Ancestry.com (indexed); NARA San Bruno (originals)

---

### 10. Overland Trail Records
- **Type:** Diaries, journals, emigrant rosters, and wagon company records documenting overland migration via the California Trail, Overland Trail, and related routes.
- **Repositories:** **California State Library** (Sacramento) and the **Bancroft Library at UC Berkeley** hold the largest manuscript collections of Gold Rush–era overland diaries and emigrant accounts. Not digitized systematically — requires on-site or correspondence research.
- **Access:** Physical/manuscript; finding aids online at each repository

---

### 11. Land Records — Federal Patents
- **Repository:** **BLM General Land Office Records** (glorecords.blm.gov) — free, searchable database of federal land patents issued in California
- **Relevance:** Miners who transitioned to farming or town settlement often filed for land. Patentees can be searched by name.
- **County deeds:** At individual county courthouses for transfers after the initial patent

---

### 12. Naturalization Records
- **Period note:** Before 1906, any court of record could naturalize — federal district courts, state courts, and county courts all did so.
- **Where held:** NARA San Bruno holds federal district court naturalization records; California State Archives and individual county courthouses hold state/county court files.
- **Access:** Partially indexed on Ancestry.com and Fold3; physical originals at repositories above

---

### 13. Military Records
- **Relevant populations:**
  - Veterans of the Mexican-American War (1846–48) who then joined the Gold Rush
  - California militia records from the 1850s
- **Repository:** **NARA** (Washington, D.C. / St. Louis) for service records and pension files; **Fold3** for digitized military records
- **Access:** Fold3 (subscription); NARA (free in-person; copies by mail/request)

---

### 14. Newspapers
- **Value:** Death notices, miner directories, arrival lists, and legal notices (estate proceedings) frequently appeared in Gold Rush–era papers.
- **Key titles:** *Alta California* (San Francisco, from 1849), *Sacramento Union* (from 1851), various Mother Lode county papers
- **Repository:** **Newspapers.com** (subscription); California State Library (microfilm and originals); UC Berkeley Bancroft Library

---

### 15. Cemetery Records
- **Repository:** **FindAGrave** (findagrave.com) — free database of cemetery inscriptions
- **Note:** Many Gold Rush miners who died in the camps were buried in unmarked or poorly documented graves; cemetery coverage is uneven for remote mining districts.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | State census 1852, county births/marriages, mining claim images, mission records, Great Registers | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census 1850/1860, ship passenger manifests, city directories, newspapers | Subscription |
| **NARA Pacific Region — San Bruno, CA** | San Francisco ship manifests, federal court naturalizations | In-person / copies |
| **California State Archives — Sacramento** | State-level court, legislative, land, and administrative records | In-person / some online |
| **California State Library — Sacramento** | Overland diaries, Gold Rush manuscripts, newspapers, Great Registers | In-person / some online |
| **Bancroft Library, UC Berkeley** | Largest Gold Rush manuscript collection in existence; diaries, memoirs, photographs | In-person; finding aids at oac.cdlib.org |
| **County courthouses** (El Dorado, Tuolumne, Calaveras, Placer, Nevada, etc.) | Mining claim registers, deed books, probate files, early marriage/birth records | In-person |
| **BLM GLO** (glorecords.blm.gov) | Federal land patents | Free online |
| **Fold3** | Military service and pension records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical California newspapers | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave** | Cemetery inscriptions | Free online |

---

## ⚠️ Critical Research Quirks

1. **1850 federal census is incomplete.** Several California county returns were destroyed. Always check the **1852 California State Census** (FamilySearch DGS 006601001, 88% indexed) first — it is often the better source for early arrivals.

2. **No statewide vital registration until 1905.** County-level birth and death registration from 1850 was inconsistently observed. Missing vital records are the norm, not an anomaly, for this period.

3. **Mining claim registers at FamilySearch are browse-only** (DGS 006601002 — 0% indexed, not full-text searchable). Plan time to browse image by image, or contact the county recorder where the ancestor mined for original registers.

4. **Port arrival registers at FamilySearch are also browse-only** (DGS 006601003 — 0% indexed). For better-indexed sea arrivals, use the NARA San Bruno manifest series via Ancestry.com instead.

5. **Great Registers begin in 1866**, not the 1850s — but they are indispensable for Gold Rush research. Every male resident who registered to vote listed birthplace, occupation, and physical description, making them a de facto directory of settled miners.

6. **Volume_search returned 14 total volumes** for 1848–1865; only the first page of 3 was retrieved above. Additional digitized volumes (11 more) exist on FamilySearch and may include county-level probate, court, and other record types not shown here.

7. **Chinese, Hispanic, and Native American residents are systematically underrepresented.** Standard U.S. civil records excluded or ignored large portions of the Gold Rush population. For Chinese immigrants, consult the [Chinese Exclusion Act case files at NARA](https://www.archives.gov); for Hispanic Californios, search pre-1848 Mission Records and Mexican land grant records at NARA and the California State Archives.

8. **The Gold Rush population was overwhelmingly transient and male.** Many miners left no California vital record at all — they arrived, mined, and either left or died without generating a local civil document. Focus first on census, mining claims, passenger manifests, and newspapers before assuming an ancestor is not documented.

---

## Recommended Search Order for a Gold Rush Ancestor

1. **1852 California State Census** (FamilySearch — 88% indexed)
2. **1850 & 1860 U.S. Federal Census** (FamilySearch / Ancestry)
3. **Ship passenger manifests** via Ancestry.com (sea arrivals) or overland records at Bancroft/State Library
4. **Mining claim registers** — county recorder level (browse-only at FamilySearch or contact county)
5. **Great Registers, 1866+** (FamilySearch — indexed) if ancestor remained in California
6. **County marriages & births** (FamilySearch — indexed, but expect gaps)
7. **Federal land patents** (BLM GLO) if ancestor settled land
8. **Newspapers** (Newspapers.com / California State Library) for death notices & mining news
9. **Naturalization records** (NARA San Bruno / county courthouses) if foreign-born
10. **Bancroft Library manuscript search** (oac.cdlib.org) for diaries, letters, or company rosters

---

*Sources: FamilySearch collections_search and volume_search results; FamilySearch Wiki, "California Genealogy — Gold Rush Records" and "Pre-Statehood Records"; Wikipedia, "California Gold Rush"; NARA Pacific Region; BLM GLO.*

Now I'll fire the full survey batch in parallel.Now I'll read the NYC genealogy wiki page returned by the search.Let me quickly check the output format template before compiling.I now have all the data needed. Here is the complete locality guide:

---

# Locality Guide: New York City, 1700s Colonial Period (1700–1799)

## Jurisdiction Overview

- **Administrative center:** New York City (Manhattan / New York County)
- **Parent jurisdiction:** Province of New York (British Crown Colony), 1664–1776; State of New York from 1777 onward
- **Political periods within the century:**
  - *British colonial rule,* 1700–1776 — NYC served as capital of New York Province and a major Atlantic commercial port
  - *Revolutionary War and British occupation,* 1776–1783 — The city fell to British forces in September 1776 and remained under military occupation until November 1783; record-keeping was disrupted
  - *Early national period,* 1783–1799 — NYC briefly served as the first federal capital (1789–1790) under the new Constitution
- **Population:** No fixture population data was returned by tool; NYC's colonial population is estimated at roughly 5,000–7,000 in 1700, growing to ~33,000 by 1790 (first federal census)
- **Economy:** Maritime trade, merchant commerce, shipping, artisan crafts, and some agriculture in outer wards
- **Ethnic composition:** Dutch (dominant into early 1700s, assimilating through the century), English, French Huguenot, African (enslaved and free), Scots-Irish, Jewish (Sephardic, from 1654)
- **Religious denominations:** Dutch Reformed (largest and oldest institutional presence), Anglican/Episcopal (Trinity Church, est. 1696), Presbyterian (First Presbyterian, est. 1716), French Reformed/Huguenot (Église du Saint-Esprit), Lutheran, Baptist, Quaker, Jewish (Congregation Shearith Israel)
- **Key historical events:** 1712 and 1741 slave uprisings (generated court records), 1765 Stamp Act protests, 1776–1783 British occupation (disrupted records; many residents fled or became Loyalists)

*(Source: Wikipedia, "New York City"; FamilySearch Wiki, "New York City Genealogy, Colonial Records")*

---

## Boundary Changes

- NYC during the 1700s encompassed **New York County** (Manhattan Island) as the urban core; the surrounding counties — Kings (Brooklyn), Queens, Richmond (Staten Island), and Westchester — were legally distinct, each with their own county-level records
- No major boundary change affected Manhattan/New York County during the 1700s
- **Before 1683:** The area was administered under the Duke of York's proprietary colony, with records held by the colonial governor; from 1683 a formal county structure was established
- **Records continuity note:** Records created under the British provincial government are held today at the **New York State Archives** (Albany). Records created at the city/county level are held by the **New York City Municipal Archives**. Search both when researching this period.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Civil registration of births, marriages, and deaths did **not** begin until 1847 (city) and 1880 (state-mandated)
- **For the 1700s:** There are **no civil vital records**. Church registers are the sole systematic substitute
- **Gaps:** Any family not affiliated with a record-keeping congregation will not appear in organized vital registers

---

### Church Records ⭐ Primary Source for This Period

| Denomination | FamilySearch Collection | Digitization Level | Language |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dutch Reformed (all NY) | [New York, Church Records, 1640–1940](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1469062) — collection 1469062 | **Indexed + images** (collection level) | Dutch into 1760s; mixed thereafter |
| Dutch Reformed (NYC only) | Image group **007733180** — 211 images, 1700–1776 | **Browse-only** — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable | Dutch — must be read in the original language |
| Anglican/Episcopal, Presbyterian, Others | Included in collection 1469062 | Indexed + images | English |

**Critical flag — Dutch Reformed registers:** The one digitized volume specifically covering NYC Dutch Reformed Church registers for 1700–1776 (image group 007733180, 211 images) is **zero percent name-indexed and not full-text searchable**. It must be browsed image by image with no name search. The entries are in **Dutch** — a researcher who cannot read Dutch must either learn to recognize key words or engage a translator/transcriber before this volume is usable.

**Other denominations with colonial-era registers:**
- **Trinity Church (Anglican/Episcopal):** Baptisms, marriages, and burials from 1697 onward; among the richest colonial church record series in NYC. Registers are published and indexed.
- **First Presbyterian Church:** Records from 1716
- **Congregation Shearith Israel (Jewish):** Records from the 1720s–1730s; held by the congregation and published
- **French Huguenot (Église du Saint-Esprit):** Records from late 1600s through the 18th century; published by the Huguenot Society

*(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "New York City Genealogy, Colonial Records")*

**Physical repositories for church records:**
- **New York Genealogical & Biographical Society (NYGBS)** — holds published transcriptions of most major colonial church registers; accessible online at nygbs.org (subscription) and via their library
- **New-York Historical Society (NYHS)** — original and microfilmed registers for several congregations
- **Individual church archives** — Trinity Church and Shearith Israel maintain their own archives

---

### Census Records

- **Federal census:** First federal census was **1790** — just outside the colonial period, but it captures families who lived through the 1700s. NYC is fully enumerated in 1790.
- **Colonial-era enumerations:** No systematic annual census. Provincial authorities conducted irregular enumerations in 1698, 1703, 1712, 1723, 1731, 1737, 1746, 1749, 1756, and 1771 — these are **manuscript head-counts**, not household schedules, and their survival and accessibility varies
- **Tax assessment lists as a substitute:** Ward-level tax lists survive for scattered years and function as census substitutes (see Tax Records below)
- **Access:** Colonial enumerations are held at the **New York State Archives**; some are transcribed in published genealogical literature

---

### Probate and Court Records ⭐ High Value

**Probate:**
- Wills for colonial New Yorkers were proved before the **Prerogative Court of the Province of New York** (the governor's court), not county surrogates
- The **New-York Historical Society** published the *Abstracts of Wills on File in the Surrogate's Office, City of New York* (19 volumes), covering 1665–1801 — this is the premier finding aid for colonial-era NYC wills. Indexed by name.
- FamilySearch collection: [New York, Probate Records, 1629–1971](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1542780) — collection 1542780 — **indexed + images**, date range covers the full colonial period

**Court records:**
- **Mayor's Court of New York City** — civil jurisdiction; case files name litigants and witnesses and can establish family relationships
- **Court of General Sessions** — criminal cases; the 1712 and 1741 slave uprising trials generated substantial documentation naming enslaved people and their enslavers
- **Court of Chancery** — equity matters, including guardianships
- Physical records: **New York State Archives** and **NYC Municipal Archives**; some published in the *Documentary History of the State of New York* (4 volumes)

---

### Land Records

- **System:** Metes-and-bounds; land distributed by royal patents and provincial grants under the British Crown
- **Record types:** Deeds, conveyances, patents, mortgages, and rent rolls. Large manorial grants existed in surrounding counties; Manhattan land was more densely subdivided through the century.
- FamilySearch collection: [New York, Land Records, 1630–1975](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078654) — collection 2078654 — **indexed + images**, covers the full target period
- **Physical repositories:** NYC Register's Office (deeds and conveyances), New York State Archives (patents and provincial grants), Library of Congress (colonial land patent records)
- **Published aids:** The *Calendar of New York Colonial Manuscripts, Indorsed Land Papers* covers 1643–1803

---

### Military Records

- **Relevant conflicts:** Queen Anne's War (1702–1713), King George's War (1744–1748), French and Indian War (1754–1763), American Revolution (1775–1783)
- **Colonial militia rolls:** Provincial militia lists name men of military age by ward and county — useful for locating male residents in years without census coverage. Held at the **New York State Archives**; some published. *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "New York City Genealogy, Tax and Military Records")*
- **Revolutionary War records:**
  - *American side:* Service and pension records at **NARA** (Record Group 93); searchable on [Fold3.com](https://www.fold3.com/)
  - *British/Loyalist side:* NYC was under British occupation 1776–1783. Many residents were Loyalists; their records (muster rolls, Loyalist Claims Commission files) are held at the **Public Record Office / The National Archives, Kew, UK** and published as the *Loyalists in the Southern Province* and related series. Claims are indexed and accessible via the **Library and Archives Canada** portal.
- **Bounty land warrants:** Post-war grants for American veterans; NARA and BLM-GLO records

---

### Cemetery Records

- **Major colonial cemeteries:**
  - *Trinity Church Churchyard* (Manhattan) — burials from 1681; indexed and published; some records online via Trinity Church archives
  - *Dutch Reformed Church burial registers* — included in image group 007733180 (browse-only, in Dutch)
  - *Congregation Shearith Israel ("Old Cemetery")* — the oldest surviving Jewish burial ground in the US (1683); published and indexed
  - *African Burial Ground* — thousands of burials from the 17th–18th centuries; excavated 1991; a National Monument; records at the **Howard University** African Burial Ground research project
- **Online coverage:** [FindAGrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com/) has entries for many Trinity and other colonial graves; coverage is incomplete for 1700s markers

---

### Newspaper Records

- **Colonial newspapers published in NYC:**
  - *New York Gazette* (1725–1744, 1747–1773) — the first NYC newspaper
  - *New York Weekly Journal* (1733–1751)
  - *New York Mercury* (1752–1783)
  - *New York Journal* (1766–1776)
- **Content value:** Marriage and death notices, runaway slave advertisements (name enslaved people and enslavers), ship arrivals, legal notices, property sales
- **Access:** [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/) (subscription) and the **Library of Congress Chronicling America** database. The NYHS and NYPL hold microfilm.
- **Runaway slave ads:** Searchable in the *Freedom on the Move* database (freedomonthemove.org) — valuable for tracing enslaved individuals' names, ages, and origins

---

### Immigration and Port Records

- **NYC as a major colonial port:** Passenger lists for the colonial period are sparse and inconsistent — systematic manifests were not required until 1820
- **What survives:** Some ships' passenger lists and port entry records exist in British colonial customs records and provincial archives; published in *Lists of Inhabitants of Colonial New York* and related volumes
- **Naturalization:** Colonial naturalizations were granted by the provincial legislature or courts; records at the **NY State Archives**. Published in *Denizations and Naturalizations in the British Colonies in America, 1607–1775*
- **Access:** NARA holds some colonial port records; many are published by the NYGBS and NYHS

---

### Tax Records

- **Types:** Provincial assessments by ward; personal property and real estate valuations
- FamilySearch collection: [New York, Tax Assessment Rolls of Real and Personal Estates, 1799–1804](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1678432) — collection 1678432 — just touches the end of the target period
- **For 1700–1798:** Tax rolls survive in the **NY State Archives** and are the best available substitute for the absent census record for most of the century
- **Substitute value:** *(Source: FamilySearch Wiki, "New York City Genealogy, Tax and Military Records")* Ward-level assessments list heads of household by year and can track residence through the century

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Collections for This Place | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Church Records 1640–1940 (indexed + images); Land Records 1630–1975 (indexed + images); Probate Records 1629–1971 (indexed + images); Tax Rolls 1799–1804; Dutch Reformed Church Registers 1700–1776 browse-only volume (007733180) | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | NY Church Records; NY Land Records; NY Probate; Revolutionary War pension files; colonial newspapers | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Revolutionary War service and pension records; Loyalist records | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Revolutionary War service, pension, and bounty land records; some colonial customs records | Free (catalog); copies by request |
| **Newspapers.com** | Colonial NYC newspapers (Gazette, Mercury, Journal) | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Trinity Church and other colonial cemetery entries (partial) | Free |
| **NYGBS (nygbs.org)** | Published transcriptions of colonial church registers; will abstracts | Subscription |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New York State Archives** (Albany, NY) | Colonial Manuscripts; provincial court records; militia rolls; land patents; colonial enumerations | In-person; some records digitized |
| **NYC Municipal Archives** (Manhattan) | City/county court records; deed books; ward records | In-person; online catalog |
| **New-York Historical Society** (Manhattan) | Church register originals; *Abstracts of Wills* (published); manuscript collections; colonial newspapers | In-person; some digitized |
| **NY Genealogical & Biographical Society** (Manhattan) | Published church record transcriptions; will indexes; genealogical manuscripts | In-person / online subscription |
| **NY Public Library, Milstein Division** | Microfilm of church records; colonial newspapers; local history | In-person |
| **Trinity Church Archives** (Manhattan) | Original Trinity Church baptism, marriage, and burial registers from 1697 | By appointment |
| **The National Archives, Kew, UK** | British colonial administrative records; Loyalist Claims Commission files | In-person / online (some digitized) |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following records exist but are **not available in any online database** as of 2026 — physical visits or mail requests to the holding repository are required:

- **Colonial provincial court files** (Mayor's Court, Court of Chancery, General Sessions case files) — NY State Archives and NYC Municipal Archives; only some are published or microfilmed
- **Ward-level tax assessment rolls before 1799** — NY State Archives; not digitized on FamilySearch
- **British military occupation records (1776–1783)** — Public Record Office, Kew; some Loyalist materials are on Ancestry/Fold3 but most case files require repository access
- **Colonial provincial enumerations (1698–1771)** — NY State Archives manuscripts; some published in secondary sources, not systematically digitized
- **Congregation Shearith Israel original registers** — held by the congregation; published abstracts available at NYGBS
- **Dutch language church registers not yet digitized** — many volumes remain on FamilySearch microfilm only (LDS film, borrowable through FHL)

---

## Known Record Losses

- **British occupation disruption (1776–1783):** A large fire in September 1776 destroyed roughly one-quarter of the city (about 500 buildings); record losses are likely but not fully documented. Many civilian residents evacuated before the occupation, taking personal papers with them.
- **No courthouse fire comparable to other jurisdictions** is documented for this period in NYC.
- **Prerogative Court records:** Some colonial-era will files are missing or damaged; the published *Abstracts of Wills* (NYHS) is the most complete index but does not fully compensate for lost originals.
- **Church record gaps:** Not every congregation maintained registers; smaller or short-lived congregations may have left no records.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with church records, not civil records.** There are no civil birth, marriage, or death records for NYC in the 1700s. Church registers — particularly Dutch Reformed and Trinity Church — are the foundational sources for vital events.

2. **Dutch Reformed registers require Dutch reading ability.** The digitized Dutch Reformed Church volume for NYC (1700–1776, image group 007733180 on FamilySearch) is browse-only and in Dutch. If your ancestor was Dutch Reformed, budget time for translation or hire a specialist.

3. **The Prerogative Court, not the county surrogate, handled colonial wills.** Do not search county-level probate indexes for wills probated before 1778 — they belong in the Prerogative Court series. The *Abstracts of Wills* (NYHS, 19 volumes) is the indexed starting point.

4. **Search the NY State Archives, not just FamilySearch.** Many colonial records — court files, tax rolls, militia lists, land patents — exist only at the NYSA in Albany. FamilySearch's NY collections are strong but not exhaustive for the colonial period.

5. **If your ancestor was a Loyalist or was under British military governance (1776–1783),** search the Loyalist Claims Commission files at The National Archives, Kew, and on Ancestry. NYC-area Loyalists who fled to Nova Scotia or England also appear in British and Canadian records.

6. **Use the *Abstracts of Wills* and published church registers as finding aids first.** The NYHS and NYGBS published extensively indexed transcriptions of colonial church and probate records in the 19th and early 20th centuries — these are faster entry points than browsing original registers.

7. **Tax rolls can stand in for absent census years.** For most of the 1700s, ward tax assessments are the best substitute for a census. They track male household heads by ward and year.

8. **Track subjects across both New York County and surrounding counties.** Families moved between Manhattan, Kings (Brooklyn), Queens, and Westchester; records follow the county of residence at the time of creation, not modern NYC borough boundaries.

9. **The FamilySearch Wiki article "New York City Genealogy"** (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/New_York_City_Genealogy) is the recommended starting reference for this jurisdiction and period.

---

*This guide is grounded in: FamilySearch collections_search (5 NY-state-level collections), volume_search (1 digitized NYC volume, 007733180), external_links_search (6 curated external resources), FamilySearch Wiki search results ("New York City Genealogy, Colonial Records" and "Tax and Military Records"), and Wikipedia ("New York City"). The wiki_place_page tool returned generic fallback content for all four sections (home, getting_started, online_records, research_tips) and is noted as a coverage gap for this jurisdiction.*

# Virginia, 1775–1783: Revolutionary War Research Guide

## Jurisdictional Context

**Standard place:** Virginia, United States (state from 1788; colony before that, so records created 1775–1783 span both colonial and early-state governance).

Virginia was one of the original thirteen colonies and, per Wikipedia, contributed **more troops than any other state** to the Continental Army. Major engagements — including the decisive Battle of Yorktown (1781) — were fought on Virginia soil. The state maintained county-level record-keeping from the 1630s, making it one of the **best-documented colonial jurisdictions** in North America.

**Critical gap to know upfront:** The 1790 federal census for Virginia was destroyed when the British burned Washington in 1814. **County personal property tax lists (1782–1850)** are the primary substitute and serve as a near-contemporary population snapshot for the war's final years.

---

## 🎖️ Military Records — The Core Sources

These are the records most directly tied to service and wartime activity, drawn from the FamilySearch Wiki ("Virginia Military Records") and tool results:

### 1. Continental Line Service Records & Muster Rolls
- **What they contain:** Proof of enlistment, rank, unit, pay, and sometimes physical descriptions.
- **Repository:** National Archives and Records Administration (NARA), **Record Group 93**.
- **Online:** Indexed and searchable on **Fold3.com** (curated external link confirmed by FamilySearch). NARA also holds originals.
- **Access level:** Fold3 is subscription-based; NARA records can be ordered or viewed in person in Washington, D.C.

### 2. FamilySearch: Virginia Military Records, 1775–1783
- **Collection ID:** 1945678
- **Date range:** 1775–1783 — directly on target.
- **URL:** https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1945678
- **Access level:** FamilySearch (free). This collection was returned as a top result filtered to exactly the Revolutionary War window.

### 3. Virginia Militia Records
- **What they contain:** Muster rolls, pay vouchers, and service documentation for men who served in the state (not Continental) forces — often including men who never joined the Continental Line.
- **Repository:** **Library of Virginia**, Richmond, VA (the primary state archive).
- **Note:** Militia service is frequently underdocumented compared to Continental service. Many men served short-term local tours that generated minimal paperwork.

### 4. Pension & Bounty-Land Warrant Applications
- **What they contain:** Veteran depositions (often rich with family detail — birthplace, residence, names of witnesses), widow's pension applications, and proof-of-service narratives. One of the most genealogically valuable Revolutionary War record types.
- **Repository:** NARA, **Record Group 15**.
- **Online:** Fold3.com indexes many of these. Originals can be ordered from NARA.
- **Why it matters:** Widow's applications often include marriage dates and children's names — family detail absent from service records alone.

### 5. Published Virginia State Navy Records
- **What they contain:** Service of men who served in Virginia's state navy (separate from the Continental Navy).
- **Status:** Published in print; check the Library of Virginia catalog and major genealogical libraries.

---

## 🏡 Bounty Land Records (Post-War, But Directly Linked to Veterans)

Virginia granted bounty lands in the **Virginia Military District of Ohio** to its Revolutionary War veterans. These records:
- **Connect veterans to land claims** and can confirm service when pension files are missing.
- **Warrant and survey records** are held at the Virginia State Library (Library of Virginia).
- Searching both the Virginia records and the Ohio land office records together is recommended for veterans who relocated west.

---

## 📋 Substitute and Supporting Record Types

Because no 1790 census exists for Virginia, these records fill the gap:

| Record Type | FamilySearch Collection | Date Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tax Records** | Virginia, Tax Records, 1782–1850 (ID: 1456723) | 1782–1850 | The 1782–1783 personal property lists are the best near-war population substitute |
| **Land, Marriage & Will Records** | Virginia, Land, Marriage, and Will Records, 1600–1900 (ID: 1734285) | 1600–1900 | Wills probated 1775–1785 may name veterans and dependents |
| **Church Records** | Virginia, Church Records, 1680–1929 (ID: 1598230) | 1680–1929 | Anglican/Episcopal vestry books and burial records; Baptist and Presbyterian registers for Dissenters |
| **County Court Records** | Virginia, County Court Records, 1650–1920 (ID: 2134567) | 1650–1920 | Order books contain appointments, oaths, guardianships, and loyalty proceedings |

All five collections are accessible free on **FamilySearch.org**.

---

## 🏛️ Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Library of Virginia** (Richmond) | Virginia militia rolls, state-level military records, county deed/will/order books, tax lists | In person + [lva.virginia.gov](https://lva.virginia.gov); many items digitized |
| **NARA** (Washington, D.C.) | Continental Army service records (RG 93), pension files (RG 15) | In person + online via Fold3 |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Five digitized Virginia collections covering military, land, church, tax, and court records (1775–1783 window confirmed) | Free |
| **Fold3.com** | Indexed military service records and pension files | Subscription |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. Federal Census and additional state collections | Subscription |
| **FindAGrave.com** | Cemetery/burial records — useful for verifying death dates and locating family plots | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Period newspapers — may carry muster notices, casualty lists, or estate notices | Subscription |

---

## ⚠️ Research Quirks & Gotchas

1. **No 1790 Virginia census exists** — use the 1782 and 1785 personal property tax lists (in the Tax Records collection, ID 1456723) as your population-era substitute. These are the closest substitute records and are confirmed available on FamilySearch.
2. **Continental vs. militia service** are documented in different repositories (NARA vs. Library of Virginia). A man who doesn't appear in NARA pension files may still have a militia service record in Richmond.
3. **County of origin matters greatly** — Virginia's county court system held the key civil records (wills, deeds, tax lists). Always identify the home county before searching, as records were created and held locally, not centrally.
4. **Dissenters (Baptists, Presbyterians, Quakers)** won't appear in Anglican vestry books. Search denominational archives separately — many Quaker records for Virginia are held at Haverford College and Swarthmore College in Pennsylvania.
5. **Loyalist families** may have fled or had property confiscated — Virginia sequestration and confiscation records (in county court order books) document wartime property seizures and can be used to trace both Patriots and Loyalists.
6. **Virginia Military District of Ohio** — if a veteran relocated to Ohio after the war, the bounty land warrant and survey records there can retroactively document Virginia service. Work both states together.
7. **The wiki_place_page tool returned only generic (non-Virginia-specific) content** for all four sections, indicating the hosted wiki pages for Virginia were unavailable in this session. For the most current Virginia-specific guidance, consult the [FamilySearch Wiki: Virginia Military Records](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Virginia_Military_Records) directly.

---

## Suggested Research Sequence

1. **Start at FamilySearch** → search the *Virginia Military Records, 1775–1783* collection (ID 1945678) by name.
2. **Check pension files** → Fold3.com or NARA RG 15 for pension/bounty-land warrant applications — widow's files are especially rich.
3. **Search the Library of Virginia** → for militia rolls and state-level service documentation.
4. **Pull tax lists** → the 1782–1783 personal property tax lists (ID 1456723) to confirm presence in a county and identify household members.
5. **Check land and probate** → wills and deeds probated during or just after the war often reference wartime transactions or debts to the Commonwealth.
6. **Broaden to county court records** → for oaths, guardianships of soldiers' orphans, and confiscation proceedings.

# 📍 Locality Research Guide: New Orleans (Orleans Parish), Louisiana — Early 1800s (c. 1800–1850)

*Sources: FamilySearch Wiki ("New Orleans Genealogy"), FamilySearch collections and volume search, Wikipedia ("New Orleans"), FamilySearch external links survey.*

---

## Jurisdictional Context

New Orleans sits at the heart of one of the most complex jurisdictional histories in the United States — and that complexity directly shapes which records exist and where they are held.

| Period | Governing Authority | Record Language |
|---|---|---|
| 1718–1763 | French colony | French |
| 1763–1800 | Spanish colony | Spanish |
| 1800–1803 | Briefly returned to France | French/Spanish |
| 1803–1812 | U.S. Territory of Orleans | French + English |
| 1812–present | State of Louisiana | English (French persists in many records) |

Louisiana is the only U.S. state built on the **Napoleonic (civil law) Code** rather than English common law. This produces record types unique to Louisiana — **successions** (not "probate"), **tutorships** (not "guardianship"), **interdictions** — and a notarial system where nearly every legal transaction was recorded by a licensed notary. The city is organized as **Orleans Parish**, not a county.

---

## ⚠️ Disaster History and Record Losses

This is the central challenge for early New Orleans research. Three categories of disaster struck:

### Fires
- **Great Fire of 1788 (Good Friday Fire):** Destroyed approximately 856 buildings in the French Quarter, along with many French colonial-era records.
- **Great Fire of 1794:** A second catastrophic fire destroyed another ~200 buildings. Together, these two fires wiped out most of the built environment from the French colonial period and severely damaged record repositories.

### Yellow Fever Epidemics
Recurring epidemics (notable outbreaks: 1796, 1817, 1822, 1832, 1837, 1839, 1847) killed enormous portions of the population. While they did not destroy records directly, they created gaps in vital event registration as institutions were overwhelmed.

### Hurricanes and Floods
Major storms struck in 1812, 1831, 1837, and 1839, with periodic flooding affecting repositories throughout the period.

---

## Records That Survived — and Their Substitutes

Despite these losses, a rich body of records *does* survive for the early 1800s. The key is knowing where to look.

---

### 1. Sacramental Registers — St. Louis Cathedral 🟡 Partial survival; partially digitized

**What they are:** Baptisms, marriages, and burials recorded by the Roman Catholic Church, which served the vast majority of New Orleans's population (French Creoles, Spanish colonists, free people of color, enslaved persons, and immigrants).

**Why they matter:** Louisiana had **no statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until 1914**. For the early 1800s, church sacramental registers are the *primary* vital records substitute. No church record = no vital event record.

**What survived the fires:** The registers of **St. Louis Cathedral** survive from **1731**, though with gaps caused by the fires. According to the FamilySearch Wiki article "New Orleans Genealogy," these registers covering baptisms, marriages, and burials are among the most significant surviving sources for this period.

**Where to find them:**
- **Archives of the Archdiocese of New Orleans** (physical repository): holds the original registers
- **FamilySearch Collection 1612905 — "Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925":** ~987,654 records with ~493,827 images. This collection spans the target period and is the primary online access point. It is **name-indexed** (searchable by name).
- Note: The separate **"Louisiana, Parish Marriages, 1837–1957"** (Collection 1798054) starts in 1837 and does **not** cover 1800–1836 — use the Church Records collection for early marriages.

**Language warning:** Records before ~1803 are in French or Spanish; records after 1803 are increasingly in English but French persists for decades. Researchers must be prepared to read the original language or obtain translations.

---

### 2. Notarial Records — The Single Most Important Surviving Source 🔴 Browse-only on FamilySearch

**What they are:** Under Louisiana's civil law system, a licensed notary recorded virtually every legal transaction: sales of property and enslaved persons, contracts, successions (estate settlements), manumissions (freedom papers), marriage contracts, partnerships, mortgages, and more.

**Why they survived:** The Notarial Archives were maintained separately from city government buildings and largely survived the fires. According to the FamilySearch Wiki article "New Orleans Genealogy," the **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** holds **over 30 million pages of notarial acts dating to 1734** — an extraordinary resource.

**FamilySearch digitized volume:** `volume_search` found one volume:
- **Image Group 004990115** — "Notarial Acts (surviving microfilm copies)," covering **1803–1820**, 167 images, in **French and English**
- ⚠️ **Browse-only: 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable.** Must be browsed image-by-image with no search capability. Researchers must be able to read French.

**Physical repository:** The **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** (New Orleans) — researchers can visit or hire a local researcher. This is the authoritative source for the full run of notarial records.

**Why this matters as a substitute:** Notarial acts frequently name family members, give ages, list relationships, describe property, and identify the parties' origins. A succession (estate settlement) can substitute for a death record and a will simultaneously. Manumission records document free people of color. These records are irreplaceable for the early 1800s.

---

### 3. Probate / Succession Records 🟢 Indexed + images on FamilySearch

**What they are:** Louisiana's civil-law equivalent of probate — inventories of estates, distributions among heirs, tutorship (guardianship) of minors. These name the deceased, heirs, and often relationships.

**FamilySearch Collection 2078341 — "Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984":**
- ~456,789 records, ~228,394 images
- Covers the target period (1800–1850)
- Name-indexed and searchable online

**Physical repository:** Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge); Orleans Parish courthouse records

---

### 4. Death Index 🟡 Index only (no images)

**FamilySearch Collection 1543892 — "Louisiana, Statewide Death Index, 1819–1964":**
- ~2,345,678 records
- Starts 1819 — does not cover 1800–1818
- **Index only: no associated images.** Useful for finding a name and date, then locating the original record elsewhere.

---

### 5. Federal Census Records 🟢 Indexed + images

Federal censuses covering New Orleans for the target period:
- **1810** (Territory of Orleans/early statehood)
- **1820, 1830, 1840, 1850**

Available on Ancestry.com (curated external link) and FamilySearch. These are name-indexed and searchable. Note: the 1800 census for the Territory of Orleans has significant gaps and partial survival.

---

### 6. Military Records 🟢 Indexed (Fold3, NARA)

New Orleans is the site of the **Battle of New Orleans (January 8, 1815)** — the final major engagement of the War of 1812. Military service records, pension files, and muster rolls for this period are held at:
- **NARA** (National Archives and Records Administration) — service records and pension files
- **Fold3.com** — digitized military records (curated external link returned by the survey)

Louisiana militia and territorial records may also be at the **Louisiana State Archives**.

---

### 7. Historical Newspapers 🟡 Partial — Newspapers.com

New Orleans had active newspapers throughout the early 1800s (*Louisiana Gazette*, *L'Ami des Lois*, *New Orleans Bee / L'Abeille de la Nouvelle-Orléans*, etc.). Newspapers are a critical substitute source when vital records are missing:
- Notices of deaths and burials
- Marriage announcements
- Estate sale notices (corroborating notarial records)
- Ship arrivals (immigration / passenger movements)

**Access:** Newspapers.com (curated external link). Also check the **Historic New Orleans Collection** and **Louisiana State University** digital newspaper archives.

---

### 8. Land Records 🟡 Partially available

Louisiana was a **federal land state** — land grants and patents from the early 1800s (post-Louisiana Purchase) are at the **BLM General Land Office (GLO)** website (glorecords.blm.gov). Spanish-era land grants are at the **Louisiana State Archives** and in the notarial records. These can place an ancestor in a specific location and reveal family relationships.

---

### 9. Cemetery Records 🟡 FindAGrave

New Orleans's famous aboveground cemeteries (St. Louis Cemetery No. 1, founded 1789; No. 2, 1823; No. 3, 1854) are significant repositories of burial information. Many are indexed at **FindAGrave.com** (curated external link). Physical inscription visits may be necessary for early, deteriorated markers.

---

## Repository Summary

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center** | 30M+ pages of notarial acts from 1734 | In-person or hired researcher |
| **Archives of the Archdiocese of New Orleans** | St. Louis Cathedral sacramental registers from 1731 | In-person; partially on FamilySearch |
| **Louisiana State Archives (Baton Rouge)** | Territorial and early state records, successions, land | In-person + some online |
| **The Historic New Orleans Collection** | Manuscripts, newspapers, maps | In-person + some online |
| **NARA (Washington / Atlanta region)** | Federal census, military, naturalization | Online (Ancestry, Fold3, FamilySearch) |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records, probate, death index, notarial microfilm | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census, some Louisiana collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military records | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical New Orleans newspapers | Subscription |

---

## FamilySearch Collections Confirmed for This Period

| Collection ID | Title | Date Range | Records | Images | Searchability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1612905 | Louisiana, Church Records | 1718–1925 | ~987,654 | ~493,827 | ✅ Name-indexed |
| 2078341 | Louisiana, Probate Records | 1756–1984 | ~456,789 | ~228,394 | ✅ Name-indexed |
| 1543892 | Louisiana, Statewide Death Index | 1819–1964 | ~2,345,678 | 0 | ✅ Index only |
| 1798054 | Louisiana, Parish Marriages | 1837–1957 | ~567,890 | ~283,945 | ✅ Name-indexed (starts 1837) |
| 004990115 | Notarial Acts (microfilm) | 1803–1820 | — | 167 | 🔴 Browse-only (French/English) |

---

## Critical Research Quirks

1. **No civil vital registration before 1914.** Church records (especially St. Louis Cathedral) are the only vital records substitute for births, marriages, and deaths in the early 1800s.
2. **The Parish Marriages collection starts in 1837** — for 1800–1836 marriages, use the Church Records collection (1612905).
3. **Louisiana uses "parishes," not "counties."** New Orleans = Orleans Parish. All records are filed at the parish level.
4. **Succession ≠ Probate.** Search for "successions" in Louisiana, not "probate," when using finding aids and indexes.
5. **Records before 1803 are in Spanish or French** — researchers must read the original language or hire a translator.
6. **The FamilySearch notarial acts volume (004990115) is browse-only** — no name index, no full-text search. Plan to browse 167 images page by page for the 1803–1820 window.
7. **The Notarial Archives are the disaster survivor par excellence** — when church or civil records are missing, the notarial record for the same event (a marriage contract, a succession) may still exist and be richer in detail.
8. **Free people of color had a distinct legal status** under both Spanish and early American law. Manumission records in the notarial archives are the primary source for documenting this community.
9. **The 1800 census for the Territory of Orleans has gaps** — treat it as incomplete.

---

## Research Strategy Summary

**Start here for the early 1800s:**
1. **St. Louis Cathedral church records** via FamilySearch Collection 1612905 — this is your vital records foundation.
2. **Louisiana Probate/Succession Records** (Collection 2078341) — names heirs and relationships.
3. **New Orleans Notarial Archives** — essential for any legal event (property, estates, manumission, contracts). Browse FamilySearch volume 004990115 for 1803–1820; contact the Notarial Archives Research Center for the rest.
4. **Federal Census** (1810–1850) on FamilySearch/Ancestry.
5. **Newspapers** via Newspapers.com and the Historic New Orleans Collection — obituaries and estate notices as vital record substitutes.
6. **Military records** via Fold3/NARA, especially for the War of 1812.
7. **FamilySearch Death Index** (1543892) for 1819+ — index only, then track the original record.

The two 1788/1794 fires destroyed much, but the **notarial archives and cathedral registers are your firewall** — they survived and between them they document most of the city's major life events. Researchers who go beyond FamilySearch's digitized holdings to contact the Notarial Archives Research Center and the Archdiocesan Archives directly will find far more than online-only research reveals.

---

*No active research project was detected, so this guide has not been persisted to research.json. If you'd like to open a project and build a research plan around a specific ancestor, just say so.*

Now I'll run the full parallel survey — population, wiki pages, collections, external links, and digitized volumes — all in one batch.The wiki search returned specific pages — let me read the Massachusetts Immigration article now.I now have all the data needed. ## 📍 Locality Research Guide: Port of Boston Immigration, 1840s
**Place:** Boston, Suffolk County, Massachusetts, United States
**Time Period:** 1840–1860
**Focus:** Tracing immigrants arriving through the Port of Boston

---

### Jurisdictional Context

Boston was incorporated as a city in 1822 and sits within **Suffolk County**, created in 1643. By the 1840s it was one of the five primary U.S. ports of entry. The decade was dominated by **Irish Famine migration** (the Great Famine began in 1845): by 1850, more than a quarter of Boston's residents were Irish-born. Immigrants also arrived from Germany, Britain, Canada, and Scandinavia. All port records for this period were generated under **federal customs authority** (not state), so the primary record trail runs through the National Archives, not Massachusetts repositories.

Massachusetts began **civil registration of vital events in 1842**, making this a transition period: some early-1840s events appear only in church or town records, while events from mid-decade onward are more consistently registered at the state level.

---

### 1. 🚢 Passenger Lists — Primary Record Class

**Federal Customs Passenger Lists (1820–1891)**
The single most important record for 1840s Boston arrivals. Under the Steerage Act of 1819, ship captains were required to file manifests at the port of entry listing every passenger.

| Source | Access Level | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **FamilySearch** — *Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891* (Collection 1784312) | ✅ **Indexed + Images** | Searchable by name; covers the full 1840s window |
| **FamilySearch** — Digitized volume `003345210` (*Passenger Lists, Port of Boston, 1840–1850*) | ⚠️ **Full-text searchable, 3% name-indexed** | 389 images; nearly browse-only for names but can be searched as full text — flag for page-by-page browsing if the indexed collection misses your ancestor |
| **Ancestry.com** | ✅ Indexed | Indexes the same NARA Record Group 36 originals; worth cross-checking as indexing differs |
| **National Archives at Boston (Waltham, MA)** — NARA Record Group 36 | 📁 Physical originals | The authoritative originals; visit or order copies through NARA |

**What 1840s lists contain:** Name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin only. Unlike post-1890s manifests, they do **not** record hometown, last residence, or U.S. contact. "Ireland" or "Germany" is the most specific origin you will typically get from the manifest itself.

**Practical tip:** Spelling of names was phonetic and inconsistent. Search by phonetic variants and browse ship-by-ship if a name search fails.

---

### 2. 📜 Naturalization Records

| Source | Access Level | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **FamilySearch** — *Massachusetts, Naturalization Records, 1790–1906* (Collection 1459213) | ✅ **Indexed** | Pre-1906 naturalizations could occur in any court — Suffolk County Superior Court, U.S. Circuit Court, U.S. District Court. This collection aggregates them. |
| **National Archives at Boston (Waltham)** | 📁 Physical | Holds federal court naturalization records; the NARA online catalog lists individual series |

**Process note:** In the 1840s, the two-step process required a **Declaration of Intention** ("first papers," filed shortly after arrival, usually stating birthplace and country) and a **Petition for Naturalization** (filed ≥2 years later). Declarations are often more genealogically rich than the final certificate and may name a specific place of origin. Both steps should be sought.

---

### 3. ✝️ Church Records — Essential for the 1840s

Because civil vital registration only began in Massachusetts in 1842 — and compliance was uneven early on — **church records are indispensable** for immigrants arriving in the first half of the decade and for any who married or had children soon after arrival.

| Source | Access Level | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **FamilySearch** — *Massachusetts, Church Records, 1630–1920* (Collection 1921657) | ✅ **Indexed** | Broad denominational coverage; search here first |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** | 📁 Physical + partial online | Holds registers for the Cathedral of the Holy Cross and St. Patrick's — the primary Irish Catholic parishes of the 1840s. Baptism registers can name the townland or parish of origin in Ireland. |
| **Other Protestant denominations** | Varies | Anglican/Episcopal, Methodist, Congregational registers are partly on FamilySearch microfilm |

**Tip:** Catholic baptismal records from this era sometimes record the parents' native parish in Ireland — far more specific than anything on the passenger list.

---

### 4. 🗳️ Civil Vital Records

Massachusetts began statewide civil registration in **1842**.

| Source | Access Level | Notes |
|--------|-------------|-------|
| **FamilySearch** — *Massachusetts, Town Vital Records, 1620–1890* (Collection 2061550) | ✅ **Indexed + Images** (~3.4 million records) | The main entry point for births, marriages, and deaths filed at the town level |
| **Massachusetts Vital Records to 1850** (published volumes) | 📖 Physical/digitized | Pre-registration era abstracts; useful for the early 1840s |
| **Massachusetts State Archives** | 📁 Physical | Holds the original bound registers from 1842 onward |

**Coverage gap warning:** The first years of civil registration (1842–1845) had incomplete compliance, especially among newly arrived immigrant communities. A missing record in the civil register does not mean the event did not occur — check church records in parallel.

---

### 5. 📰 The Pilot "Missing Persons" Columns — Unique Resource

*The Boston Pilot*, the Irish Catholic newspaper, published **"Missing Persons" advertisements** from the 1830s through the 1920s. Families in Ireland and Irish-American communities placed notices seeking relatives who had emigrated. These have been digitized and indexed at **[infowanted.bc.edu](http://infowanted.bc.edu)** (Boston College). Entries often state the county of origin in Ireland and the approximate year of arrival — invaluable when other records are silent.

---

### 6. 🏛️ Federal Census Records

The **1850 U.S. Federal Census** is the earliest census to list all household members by name with birthplace. For 1840s arrivals it is the first major landmark:

- The **1840 census** lists only the head of household by name (others by age/sex tally) — not useful for individual immigrants unless they were already in Boston before arrival.
- The **1850 census** lists name, age, sex, birthplace (country only), and occupation for every person. If your ancestor arrived in the 1840s, the 1850 census documents their establishment in Boston.
- **Mortality schedules** (1850 census) record deaths in the year prior to enumeration — useful for immigrants who died shortly after arrival.

Both are indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry.

---

### 7. Additional Record Classes to Consider

| Record Type | Where to Look | Relevance |
|-------------|--------------|-----------|
| **City directories** (Boston, 1840s–1850s) | Boston Public Library; Ancestry.com | Locate an immigrant in a specific ward; track residence year-by-year |
| **Almshouse / Pauper records** | Massachusetts State Archives; City of Boston Archives | Famine arrivals in dire poverty appear in poorhouse admission registers, which often note birthplace |
| **State alien passenger lists** (1848–1891) | Massachusetts State Archives | Massachusetts enacted its own alien passenger law in 1848, requiring ships to report foreign-born passengers; these may supplement federal lists |
| **Cemetery records** | Find A Grave; local Catholic cemeteries (e.g., Holy Cross Cemetery) | Death dates help anchor chronologies |
| **Suffolk County probate records** | FamilySearch — *Massachusetts, Probate Records, 1635–1991* (Collection 1678901) | Useful once an immigrant was established; inventories and letters of administration can name kin |
| **Historical newspapers** | Newspapers.com; Boston Public Library digital collections | Arrival notices, obituaries, and community news |

---

### 8. Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|-----------|--------------|--------|
| **National Archives at Boston** (Waltham, MA) | NARA RG 36 passenger lists; federal court naturalizations | In-person; some records online via Ancestry/FamilySearch |
| **Massachusetts State Archives** (Boston) | Civil vital records from 1842; state alien passenger lists 1848+ | In-person; some digitized via FamilySearch |
| **Boston Public Library** | City directories; historical newspapers; local history collections | In-person and online |
| **Archdiocese of Boston Archives** | Catholic parish registers from the 1840s onward | By appointment |
| **New England Historic Genealogical Society (NEHGS)** | Vital records abstracts; church transcripts; compiled genealogies | Membership; Boston office |
| **FamilySearch.org** | Digitized passenger lists, naturalization, vital, and church records | Free online |
| **Ancestry.com** | Overlapping passenger list indexes; census; city directories | Subscription |

---

### Recommended Search Sequence

1. **Start with passenger lists** — FamilySearch Collection 1784312 (*Massachusetts, Passenger Lists, 1820–1891*); cross-check on Ancestry. If you don't find a hit, browse volume `003345210` using full-text search.
2. **Pull the 1850 census** to confirm where the immigrant settled and who else was in the household.
3. **Search naturalization records** — look for both the Declaration of Intention (early, richer detail) and the final petition.
4. **Search church records** for baptisms, marriage, and burial — especially Catholic registers if the family was Irish.
5. **Check The Pilot "Missing Persons"** at infowanted.bc.edu.
6. **Search Massachusetts civil vital records** from 1842 onward for births, marriages, and deaths of the immigrant and immediate family.
7. **Consult city directories** year-by-year to build an address trail and occupation timeline.

---

### Key Quirks & Cautions

- **1840s passenger lists record country of origin only** — "Ireland" not "County Cork." The naturalization declaration and Catholic baptism registers are your best chances at a more specific origin.
- **Massachusetts civil registration began in 1842**, but early compliance was patchy for immigrant communities. Always check church records alongside civil registers for 1842–1848 events.
- **State alien passenger lists (from 1848)** at the Massachusetts State Archives are separate from and in addition to the federal customs lists — they may contain names missing from the federal series.
- **Pre-1906 naturalizations can appear in any court of record** — don't limit your search to federal courts alone. Suffolk County Superior Court naturalizations are substantial and partially indexed.
- **The Pilot "Missing Persons" database** is a unique resource with no equivalent for other immigrant groups — use it early, not as a last resort.
