---

# 🗺️ Locality Research Guide
## Native American Families in Indian Territory (Oklahoma) — Mid-1800s (ca. 1830–1870)

---

## Jurisdictional Context

Oklahoma was **not a U.S. state during your target period**. The region carried two overlapping designations:

| Period | Jurisdiction | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1830–1890 | **Indian Territory** (unorganized) | No territorial legislature; governed by tribal nations and the federal Bureau of Indian Affairs |
| 1890–1907 | **Oklahoma Territory** (western half) + **Indian Territory** (eastern half) | Split jurisdiction after Organic Act of 1890 |
| 1907+ | **Oklahoma** (state) | Statehood merged both territories |

**Critical implication for records:** Because Indian Territory had no standard U.S. civil infrastructure in the mid-1800s, the primary record-creators were (1) the federal government via treaties and the Bureau of Indian Affairs, (2) the tribal governments themselves, and (3) Christian missionaries. Standard state-level record series (vital registration, county deeds, county courts) simply do not exist here for the 1830–1870 window. Research must pivot to the alternative record universe below.

**The Five Civilized Tribes** — Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek (Muscogee), and Seminole — were relocated to Indian Territory under the Indian Removal Act of 1830 via the Trail of Tears. They are the primary focus of most mid-1800s Oklahoma Native American genealogy. Other tribes (Osage, Quapaw, Potawatomi, Shawnee, and more) also resided in the Territory under separate treaty arrangements.

---

## Available Records & Repositories

### 1. 🏛️ Tribal Enrollment Rolls *(Primary Resource)*

**What they are:** Government-compiled lists of tribal members, created for purposes of annuity payments, land allotment, and treaty obligations. Multiple rolls exist across the mid-to-late 1800s.

**Key rolls for your period:**

| Roll | Tribe | Year | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Old Settler Cherokee Roll | Cherokee | 1851 | Lists Cherokees who migrated west before forced removal |
| Drennen Roll | Cherokee | 1852 | Major census of Cherokee Nation members |
| Armstrong Roll | Choctaw & Chickasaw | 1831 | Early post-removal enumeration |
| Duval Roll | Creek (Muscogee) | 1832 | Post-removal Creek census |
| Dawes Rolls (Final Rolls) | All Five Tribes | 1898–1914 | *Post-period but essential* — the gateway record for tracing backward; lists name, age, blood quantum, tribal affiliation for 101,000+ individuals |

**FamilySearch collection:** **Oklahoma, Indian Censuses and Rolls, 1851–1959** (Collection ID: 1987432) — 567,890 indexed records with images. This overlaps your target period directly. Search at: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1987432

**FamilySearch volume:** **Tribal Enrollment Cards, Indian Territory, 1840–1870** — 940 images, **99% name-indexed**, full-text searchable. This volume is the most directly targeted digitized source for your period and can be searched by name on FamilySearch. (Image Group: 002298871)

**National Archives (NARA):** The Dawes Commission records, earlier rolls, and related allotment case files are held at **NARA Southwest Region, Fort Worth, TX** (Record Group 75). This is the single most important physical repository for Indian Territory records.

> ⚠️ **Research Quirk:** The Dawes Rolls classify individuals into categories — Full Blood, Mixed Blood, Freedmen, and Intermarried White — and an ancestor may only appear in one category. Freedmen were formerly enslaved persons held by tribal members; their descendants have separate enrollment histories. Confirm which roll type applies to your ancestor before searching.

---

### 2. 📜 Treaty Records

Federal treaties negotiated between the U.S. government and tribal nations frequently contain **schedules of signatories, tribal leaders, and population counts**. For the mid-1800s, key treaties include:

- **Treaty of New Echota (1835)** — Cherokee removal treaty; signatories listed
- **Treaty of Dancing Rabbit Creek (1830)** — Choctaw removal
- **Treaty of Doaksville (1837)** — Chickasaw/Choctaw merger
- **Fort Gibson treaties (various, 1833–1866)** — Multiple tribes

**Where to find them:** Digitized treaty texts are at the **Avalon Project (Yale Law School)** and the **Oklahoma Historical Society**. NARA holds the original treaty files in Record Group 11 (General Records of the U.S. Government).

---

### 3. 🏢 Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) Agency Records

The BIA maintained extensive records through its field agencies in Indian Territory. These include:

- **Annuity payment rolls** — annual lists of tribal members entitled to treaty payments; an excellent substitute for vital records
- **Correspondence files** — letters naming individuals in land, legal, and family disputes
- **School and educational records** — tribal schools and BIA boarding schools
- **Census records** — Indian agents conducted periodic censuses distinct from federal enumerations

**Where held:** **NARA, Southwest Region, Fort Worth, TX** (Record Group 75 — Bureau of Indian Affairs). Many of these records are not yet digitized and require in-person or NARA request access.

> ⚠️ **Research Quirk:** Indian Territory was **excluded from the 1840, 1850, 1860, and 1870 U.S. federal censuses**. Standard federal census research does not apply here. Use BIA annuity rolls and tribal censuses as direct substitutes for those decades. The first federal census covering Indian Territory was **1900**.

---

### 4. ✝️ Missionary & Church Records

Christian missionaries arrived in Indian Territory from the 1820s onward — Baptist, Methodist, Presbyterian, and Moravian denominations all maintained active missions. Their registers recorded:

- Baptisms, marriages, and burials of converts
- School enrollment (mission schools)
- Community membership lists

**FamilySearch collection:** **Oklahoma, Mission and Church Records, 1820–1965** (Collection ID: 1654321) — 123,456 indexed records with images. Search at: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1654321

**Physical repositories:** Denominational archives hold originals. The **American Baptist Historical Society**, **Presbyterian Historical Society** (Philadelphia), and **Methodist Archives** (Drew University) all hold relevant records.

---

### 5. 🌾 Land Records

Land in Indian Territory was held communally by tribal nations until allotment under the Dawes Commission. Pre-allotment mid-1800s land records reflect **tribal land grants and improvements** rather than U.S. patent deeds.

**FamilySearch collection:** **Oklahoma and Indian Territory, Early Land Records, 1834–1907** (Collection ID: 1541872) — 234,567 records with 117,283 images. Covers your target period. Search at: https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1541872

**Post-allotment (1887+):** The Dawes allotment files at NARA Fort Worth document individual land assignments and contain family relationship information useful for backward research.

---

### 6. ⚖️ Tribal Court & Government Records

The Five Civilized Tribes maintained their own governments, laws, and courts throughout the mid-1800s. Tribal records include:

- Council minutes and legislative acts
- Tribal court records (citizenship disputes, estate matters)
- Citizenship enrollment records (tribal-level, distinct from Dawes rolls)

**Where held:**
- **Cherokee Nation Cultural Resource Center & Cherokee National Archives** — Tahlequah, OK
- **Choctaw Nation of Oklahoma Archives** — Durant, OK
- **Chickasaw Nation Division of History and Culture** — Sulphur, OK
- **Muscogee (Creek) Nation Archives** — Okmulgee, OK
- **Seminole Nation Museum and Archives** — Wewoka, OK

These tribal archives hold records not found anywhere else and are underutilized by researchers. Contact each directly for access policies.

---

### 7. 🎖️ Military Records

Many Native Americans served in the Civil War (1861–1865) on both Union and Confederate sides — the Five Civilized Tribes were formally allied with the Confederacy. Service records and pension files name individuals and family members.

**Where held:** **NARA, Washington, D.C.** — Civil War service records for Indian units. **Fold3.com** has digitized many pension files. The Oklahoma Historical Society also holds state-level military records.

---

### 8. 📰 Newspapers & Published Sources

Tribal newspapers provide community-level genealogical detail unavailable elsewhere:

- **Cherokee Phoenix / Cherokee Advocate** — published from 1828, names community members in announcements, legal notices, and obituaries
- **Indian Journal** (Creek Nation) — from 1876

**Where held:** Oklahoma Historical Society; partial digitization at **Newspapers.com** and the **Chronicling America** project (Library of Congress, free).

---

### 9. 🔗 Key Online Repositories

| Repository | What's There | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** (familysearch.org) | Indian Censuses & Rolls, Land Records, Mission Records, Tribal Enrollment Cards | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | Dawes Rolls, BIA records, Cherokee censuses | Subscription |
| **Fold3.com** | Military records, pension files, some BIA records | Subscription |
| **NARA Online (archives.gov)** | Access to Records Group 75; some digitized via OPA | Free; some records on request |
| **Oklahoma Historical Society** (okhistory.org) | Territorial records, newspapers, photographs | Free online catalog; some digitized |
| **Newberry Library** (newberry.org) | One of the nation's largest collections of Native American records | In-person; some finding aids online |

---

## Research Strategy & Quirks

1. **Start with the Dawes Rolls, then work backward.** Even though they post-date your target period (1898–1914), the Dawes Rolls are the anchor record for Five Tribes research. They establish blood quantum and tribal affiliation, which then tells you which earlier rolls to search.

2. **Indian Territory was invisible to federal census-takers 1840–1890.** Do not assume an ancestor is lost because they don't appear in an 1850 or 1860 census. Pivot immediately to BIA annuity rolls and tribal censuses.

3. **Names are a significant challenge.** Mid-1800s records frequently recorded Native Americans under anglicized names. The same individual may appear under a tribal name in one record and an English name in another. Compile all name variants before searching.

4. **Blood quantum categories create siloed records.** An ancestor enrolled as "Mixed Blood" will not appear in the "Full Blood" rolls. If a search comes up empty, try all roll categories.

5. **Tribal archives are irreplaceable.** For Cherokee, Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, and Seminole ancestors, the tribal archives in Oklahoma hold records — especially citizenship papers and council documents — that have no equivalent elsewhere and are not on FamilySearch or Ancestry.

6. **FamilySearch wiki coverage gap:** The FamilySearch Research Wiki did not return Oklahoma-specific Native American research pages during this survey. Consult the FamilySearch wiki article **"Oklahoma Native American Records"** directly, which covers Dawes Rolls, earlier enrollment rolls, and missionary records in detail (sourced via wiki search: https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Oklahoma_Native_American_Records).

---

## Digitization Summary

| Record Type | Availability |
|---|---|
| Tribal Enrollment Cards (1840–1870) | ✅ **Indexed + images** — 99% name-indexed on FamilySearch |
| Indian Censuses & Rolls (1851–1959) | ✅ **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch collection 1987432 |
| Early Land Records (1834–1907) | ✅ **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch collection 1541872 |
| Mission & Church Records (1820–1965) | ✅ **Indexed + images** — FamilySearch collection 1654321 |
| BIA Agency Records / Annuity Rolls | ⚠️ **Microfilm / physical** — largely undigitized; NARA Fort Worth |
| Tribal Government / Court Records | ⚠️ **Physical only** — at individual tribal archives in Oklahoma |
| Treaty records | ✅ **Online** — Avalon Project, NARA digital |
| Tribal newspapers | ⚠️ **Partial digitization** — Chronicling America, OHS |
| Military / pension files | ✅ **Indexed + images** — Fold3, NARA OPA |

---

**Bottom line:** The most productive starting path is: (1) search FamilySearch's **Indian Censuses and Rolls** collection and the **Tribal Enrollment Cards** volume for your target period, (2) use the Dawes Rolls to anchor identity and work backward through earlier rolls, and (3) contact the relevant tribal nation's archive directly for records that have never been microfilmed or digitized. NARA Fort Worth is essential for any serious research in BIA records.

---

## 📋 Pennsylvania Quaker Community Records Guide
**Place:** Pennsylvania, United States | **Period:** Late 1700s (ca. 1750–1800) | **Focus:** Religious Meeting Records

---

### Jurisdictional Context

Pennsylvania was founded in 1682 by William Penn as a Quaker (Religious Society of Friends) haven. By the late 1700s, Quakers were concentrated most densely in **Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, and Delaware counties** — these are your highest-priority research jurisdictions. Quaker records are especially critical for this period because **Pennsylvania had no statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until 1906**. Meeting records are often the *only* source for vital events in this era.

---

### 🏆 Primary Record Type: Quaker Meeting Records

Quaker meetings maintained exceptionally meticulous records — far more detailed than most other denominations of the period. The meeting hierarchy matters for research:

| Meeting Level | What It Recorded |
|---|---|
| **Monthly Meeting** | Births, deaths, marriages, certificates of removal, disownments, minutes of business |
| **Quarterly Meeting** | Oversight of several monthly meetings; transfer records |
| **Yearly Meeting** | Philadelphia Yearly Meeting governed all PA Friends; broad policy records |

**Key record types within meetings:**
- **Birth & death registers** — often the only source for these events in this period
- **Marriage certificates** — uniquely valuable because they were signed by *every attendee*, giving you a snapshot of the entire community present
- **Certificates of removal** — issued when a Friend moved to a new meeting; these track migration and name the originating and receiving meetings, making them essential for tracing geographic movement
- **Disownment records** — document members expelled (often for marrying outside the faith, military service, or other violations); name the individual and frequently family members
- **Minutes** — record committee appointments, family disputes, and community matters that name individuals not in other record types

---

### 🗂️ Primary Repositories

#### 1. Friends Historical Library — Swarthmore College
📍 500 College Avenue, Swarthmore, PA 19081
🌐 [www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library](https://www.swarthmore.edu/friends-historical-library)
The **largest single collection of American Quaker records** anywhere. Holds original meeting minutes, registers, and certificates for Pennsylvania monthly and quarterly meetings. Access is by appointment; many holdings have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.

#### 2. Quaker & Special Collections — Haverford College
📍 370 Lancaster Avenue, Haverford, PA 19041
🌐 [www.haverford.edu/library/special-collections](https://www.haverford.edu/library/special-collections)
Second major repository; strong in Philadelphia Yearly Meeting records and Quaker family papers.

#### 3. FamilySearch (microfilm and digital)
FamilySearch has microfilmed many Pennsylvania monthly meeting minutes and registers. One digitized volume is confirmed online for the 1750–1800 window:

> **Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes (1750–1800)**
> Image Group: 001188322 | 145 images
> ⚠️ **Browse-only** — 0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable. Must be examined image by image.

#### 4. Pennsylvania State Archives
📍 350 North Street, Harrisburg, PA 17120
Holds supplementary county-level records (land, probate, tax) that cross-reference with meeting records.

---

### 📚 Published Finding Aid — Start Here

**William Wade Hinshaw, *Encyclopedia of American Quaker Genealogy*, 6 volumes**
This is the single most important tool for Pennsylvania Quaker research. Hinshaw abstracted monthly meeting records from Pennsylvania and other states into an indexed, searchable format. Check your local genealogical library or the Allen County Public Library (which holds a comprehensive copy). FamilySearch Wiki's *Pennsylvania Church Records* article specifically names this as the primary starting point for Quaker research.

---

### 🔍 FamilySearch Indexed Collections (1750–1800 overlap)

| Collection | ID | Records | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985 | [1401638](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638) | ~1.2M records, 607K images | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994 | [1417683](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683) | ~893K records | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950 | [1571957](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957) | ~312K records | Indexed + images |
| Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900 | [1502909](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909) | ~199K records | Indexed, no images |

> ⚠️ **"Church and Town Records, 1708–1985"** is your best first search on FamilySearch for Quaker vital events — it encompasses many Pennsylvania denomination records including Friends meetings. However, Quaker-specific coverage within it is uneven by county; always follow up at the physical repositories.

---

### 📄 Complementary Record Types for This Period

Because meeting records may have gaps, use these in parallel:

| Record Type | Coverage / Notes |
|---|---|
| **Land & deed records** | County courthouses; Quakers were active property owners; deeds name neighbors and kin |
| **Probate records** | County level; wills name heirs and sometimes Quaker witnesses; FamilySearch collection 1417683 covers back to 1683 |
| **Tax records** | Pennsylvania provincial and county tax lists (1760s–1790s) survive; useful for locating individuals between records |
| **First U.S. Census (1790)** | Names heads of household only, but places a family in a specific county |
| **Pennsylvania Provincial records** | Pre-statehood records at the Pennsylvania State Archives |
| **Military exemption records** | Quakers were conscientious objectors; exemption filings sometimes survive in county or state records |
| **Historical newspapers** | *Pennsylvania Gazette* and Philadelphia-area papers published Quaker meeting notices and death notices |

---

### ⚠️ Key Research Quirks

1. **No civil registration until 1906.** Meeting records *are* the vital records for this period. Do not expect to find birth or death certificates.
2. **Search by county, not just the individual meeting.** Meeting names changed, merged, and divided. Know which monthly meeting served the county where your ancestor lived.
3. **Certificates of removal are migration tools.** When an ancestor disappears from one meeting, search receiving meetings in the direction of known migration (westward into Lancaster, York, and later Ohio).
4. **Disownment does not mean departure.** Disowned Friends sometimes remained in the community and continued appearing in land and probate records alongside active members.
5. **Marriage records are doubly useful.** The witness list on a Quaker marriage certificate can identify siblings, parents, and neighbors — treat it as a community census.
6. **Hinshaw first, original records second.** Start with the Hinshaw abstracts to identify which monthly meeting holds the record, then request the original from Friends Historical Library or FamilySearch microfilm.
7. **Volume digitization gap.** Only one digitized volume (Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes) was confirmed by FamilySearch for 1750–1800 — and it is browse-only. Most monthly meeting registers for this period remain on **microfilm only** and must be ordered through FamilySearch or consulted at Swarthmore/Haverford.

---

### 📌 Recommended Research Sequence

1. **Search Hinshaw's *Encyclopedia*** for your ancestor's name to identify which meeting holds the record
2. **Search FamilySearch "Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985"** (collection 1401638)
3. **Browse Philadelphia Yearly Meeting Minutes** (image group 001188322) if your ancestor was in the Philadelphia area
4. **Contact Friends Historical Library** (Swarthmore) to request specific monthly meeting minutes and registers
5. **Check county probate and land records** to corroborate and extend the record trail
6. **Consult the 1790 Federal Census** to locate the family geographically

---

> **Source note:** Record-type descriptions and repository information are drawn from the FamilySearch Wiki article *Pennsylvania Church Records* (Quaker Records and Published Quaker Abstracts sections) and Wikipedia's *Quakers in Pennsylvania* article. Collection record counts are from FamilySearch `collections_search`. Volume digitization status is from `volume_search`.

---

If you'd like to narrow to a specific county (Philadelphia, Chester, Bucks, Delaware, etc.) or move to building a research plan targeting specific searches, just say the word.

# Locality Research Guide
## Pittsburgh / Allegheny County, Pennsylvania — Late 1800s Steel Industry
### Target period: c. 1860–1910

---

## Jurisdictional Context

**Pittsburgh** (standardPlace: *Pittsburgh, Allegheny, Pennsylvania, United States*) is the county seat of **Allegheny County**, formed in 1788 from Washington and Westmoreland Counties. Pittsburgh was already a commercial and manufacturing hub by mid-century; the post-Civil War decades saw explosive growth in iron and steel, attracting massive immigrant labor flows — Irish, German, Polish, Slovak, Croatian, and Italian workers poured into the city and mill towns along the Monongahela and Allegheny rivers. This demographic reality shapes which records survive and where they are held: **ethnic Catholic parishes, naturalization courts, and a few industrial archives** are the front line of research for the period.

**Key jurisdictional fact:** Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide civil registration of births and deaths until **1906**. For the entire late-1800s target window, vital records are held at the county or church level — not the state level.

---

## 1. 🏭 Occupational & Industrial Records — The Priority Tier

These are the records specific to your industrial research focus. None are digitized online.

### Company Employment Records
- **Heinz History Center** (Western Pennsylvania Historical Society), Pittsburgh — holds fragmentary employment records from Carnegie Steel and successor companies, payroll ledgers, and personnel files. Coverage is uneven; many records did not survive corporate mergers.
- **Rivers of Steel National Heritage Area Archives**, Homestead, PA — preserves records from U.S. Steel and affiliated mills, especially for the Homestead and Braddock works. Includes some foreman's logs and departmental rosters.
- **Access:** Physical visit required. Neither collection is digitized or name-indexed online. Contact each repository before visiting to confirm holdings for specific mills or time windows.

### Union Records
- The **Amalgamated Association of Iron, Steel and Tin Workers** was the dominant union in Pittsburgh-area mills through the 1892 Homestead Strike. Membership rolls survive in scattered form at the Heinz History Center and at the AFL-CIO archives (Silver Spring, MD).
- **Access:** Physical only; not digitized.

### State Factory Inspector Reports
- Pennsylvania's Bureau of Industrial Statistics published **annual factory inspector reports** beginning in 1872, listing establishments, workforce counts, and sometimes individual worker grievances or accident victims.
- Held at: **Pennsylvania State Archives**, Harrisburg (Record Group 15 — Department of Labor and Industry).
- **Access:** Physical; some volumes available on Google Books and HathiTrust for selected years.

### Federal Census — Manufacturing & Industry Schedules
- The **1880 Census Supplemental Schedules** include a **Manufacturers Schedule** that lists industrial establishments by county, with number of employees, wages, and products — an indispensable snapshot of Pittsburgh's mills.
- Held at: **NARA** and microfilmed by FamilySearch/Ancestry.
- **Mortality Schedules** (1850–1880) record individuals who died in the year preceding each census, sometimes noting occupation and cause — relevant for tracing workers killed in mill accidents.

### The Pittsburgh Survey (1907–1908)
- A landmark sociological study of Pittsburgh's working class, published in 6 volumes. Contains occupational data, photographs, and neighborhood-level breakdowns of worker populations. Not a genealogical record per se, but invaluable context.
- Digitized and freely available via **HathiTrust**.

---

## 2. 📋 Federal Census Records

| Census | Notes | Access |
|--------|-------|--------|
| 1860 | Lists occupation; free population | FamilySearch / Ancestry — indexed + images |
| 1870 | Lists occupation, birthplace, real/personal estate value | FamilySearch / Ancestry — indexed + images |
| 1880 | Lists occupation, birthplace of parents; detailed | FamilySearch / Ancestry — indexed + images |
| **1890** | **Destroyed by fire (1921) — does not exist** | Substitute with city directories and Allegheny County tax records |
| 1900 | Lists occupation, immigration year, years in U.S., citizenship status | FamilySearch / Ancestry — indexed + images |
| 1910 | Lists occupation, industry, employer/employee/own-account | FamilySearch / Ancestry — indexed + images |

⚠️ **The missing 1890 census is the biggest gap for this period.** Use Pittsburgh city directories (see §5) and Allegheny County tax assessments as substitutes for locating families in that decade.

---

## 3. ✝️ Vital Records

**Statewide civil registration began 1906.** For the late 1800s target window:

### Allegheny County Births & Deaths (1870–1905)
- Allegheny County began local registration of births and deaths in **1870**, well ahead of the statewide mandate — this is a major asset.
- Held at: **Allegheny County Health Department** and on microfilm at FamilySearch.
- FamilySearch digitized collection: **Pennsylvania, Death Certificates, 1906–1968** (4,218,763 records, fully indexed + images) — note this begins at 1906 and covers deaths of workers who survived into the 20th century.

### Marriage Records
- Marriage licenses from **1885** are at the Allegheny County Courthouse.
- FamilySearch has three overlapping Pennsylvania marriage collections that cover this period:
  - **Pennsylvania, County Marriages, 1885–1950** — 524,189 records, indexed + images
  - **Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950** — 312,045 records, indexed + images
  - **Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900** — 198,765 records, indexed (no images)

### Church Records (Essential for this period)
Catholic ethnic parishes are **the primary vital record source** for immigrant steel workers before 1906. They recorded baptisms, marriages, and burials independently.
- Slovak, Polish, Croatian, Italian, and Irish parishes each served distinct communities.
- **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** holds original registers; many have been microfilmed by FamilySearch.
- FamilySearch collection: **Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985** — 1,245,830 records, 607,220 images, indexed + images.
- ⚠️ Many Slovak, Polish, and Croatian parish registers are in those languages — the researcher should be prepared to translate or seek assistance.

---

## 4. 📜 Naturalization Records

Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in **any court of record** — federal, state, or local. For Pittsburgh-area immigrants:

- **Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas** naturalization records (1798–1930) are held at the **National Archives at Philadelphia** (NARA Mid-Atlantic Region). These are the primary source.
- Petitions typically name country of origin, port/date of arrival, and witnesses — extremely useful for linking workers to immigrant communities.
- Some are indexed on **Ancestry.com** (Pennsylvania Naturalization Records) and **Fold3**.
- ⚠️ Many immigrant workers naturalized in multiple stages (first papers / declaration of intent, then final papers) — search for both.

---

## 5. 📖 City Directories

Pittsburgh city directories begin in **1813** and are an exceptional research tool for the late 1800s, especially to bridge the 1890 census gap. They list:
- Name, occupation, employer, and home address
- Sometimes list ward or neighborhood

**Held at:**
- **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** — strongest physical collection; directories from 1813 onward.
- **Heinz History Center library**
- Many years digitized and searchable at **Ancestry.com** (U.S. City Directories) and freely browsable at the **Internet Archive**.

---

## 6. ⛪ Ethnic Community & Immigration Records

Given the heavily immigrant workforce:

| Record Type | Repository | Notes |
|-------------|------------|-------|
| Passenger arrival lists | NARA / Ancestry / FamilySearch | Port of Baltimore & Philadelphia most common for Pittsburgh-bound immigrants; Castle Garden/Ellis Island for later arrivals |
| Naturalization petitions | NARA Philadelphia | Allegheny Co. Court of Common Pleas, 1798–1930 |
| Ethnic fraternal society records | Heinz History Center | Slovak, Polish, Croatian lodges; membership rolls, death benefits |
| Ethnic newspapers | Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh | *Jednota* (Slovak), *Robotnik* (Polish), *Narodni Noviny* (Slovak) — obituaries, community notices |

---

## 7. 🏛️ Probate & Land Records

- **Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994** — FamilySearch collection with 893,214 records and 446,607 images, indexed + images. Covers Allegheny County wills, inventories, and estate files. Probate for a mill worker may be sparse or absent, but guardianship files for minor children of deceased workers do appear.
- Land deeds and tax assessments are held at the **Allegheny County Recorder of Deeds**; some are microfilmed by FamilySearch.

---

## 8. ⚔️ Military Records

- Civil War service records and pension files (pre-1865 service) are at NARA and searchable on **Fold3** and **Ancestry**.
- Many immigrant steel workers arrived post-Civil War; their first U.S. military encounter would be **WWI draft registration (1917–1918)**, which is outside this guide's window but is a powerful anchor for men born in the 1870s–1890s.

---

## 9. 📰 Newspapers

Pittsburgh was well served by the press. Mill accidents, union events, strikes (especially the 1892 Homestead Strike), obituaries, and naturalization lists appeared regularly.
- **Pittsburgh Post-Gazette** and predecessor papers — some digitized on **Newspapers.com** and **GenealogyBank**.
- Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh holds physical and microfilm runs.

---

## 10. 🗓️ Summary — Research Sequence for a Pittsburgh Steel Worker

1. **Start with the census** — 1880 and 1900 are richest for occupation and nativity data. Note the 1890 gap.
2. **Substitute city directories** for the 1890s — Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh and Ancestry.
3. **Locate the ethnic parish** — determine the worker's nationality and find the corresponding Catholic (or Lutheran/Orthodox) congregation. Church registers are the best vital record source before 1906.
4. **Search naturalization records** at NARA Philadelphia — Allegheny County Court of Common Pleas, 1870–1906.
5. **Check Heinz History Center and Rivers of Steel archives** in person for any surviving company employment or union records for the specific mill.
6. **Search passenger lists** to establish immigration date and port (needed to anchor the family timeline).
7. **Check FamilySearch collections** — Pennsylvania Church and Town Records, County Marriages, and Probate Records are all indexed and searchable online.

---

## Key Repositories at a Glance

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|------------|--------------|--------|
| **Heinz History Center** (WPHS), Pittsburgh | Company records, union records, city directories, ethnic newspapers, photographs | In-person; some finding aids online |
| **Rivers of Steel Archives**, Homestead | U.S. Steel / mill records for Homestead, Braddock | In-person; contact ahead |
| **Carnegie Library of Pittsburgh** | City directories (1813–), local history, newspapers | In-person; some digital |
| **NARA Philadelphia (Mid-Atlantic)** | Allegheny County naturalizations, federal court records, census schedules | In-person; some indexed on Ancestry/Fold3 |
| **Catholic Diocese of Pittsburgh Archives** | Original parish registers for Catholic ethnic parishes | In-person; many microfilmed by FamilySearch |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives, Harrisburg** | Factory inspector reports, state-level records | In-person; some digital |
| **FamilySearch.org** | PA Church & Town Records, County Marriages, Probate Records, Death Certificates (1906+), census | Online, free, indexed |
| **Ancestry.com** | U.S. City Directories, census, naturalization indexes, PA vital records | Subscription |
| **Internet Archive / HathiTrust** | Pittsburgh Survey, factory inspector reports, city directory scans | Free online |

---

## Coverage Gaps to Note

- **Population data** was not returned for this place by the survey tool — consult published census reports (U.S. Census Bureau) for Allegheny County population figures.
- **`volume_search`** is not available in this environment, so browse-only digitized volumes on FamilySearch may exist that weren't surfaced here — browse FamilySearch's Allegheny County catalog directly.
- **Company employment records are fragmentary and not digitized** — survival depended on whether the company's successor preserved them through corporate mergers and the eventual collapse of the steel industry in the 1980s.
- **The FamilySearch wiki** returned a generic page rather than a Pennsylvania-specific article for all four sections. For authoritative Pennsylvania research guidance, visit [FamilySearch Wiki: Pennsylvania Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Genealogy) directly.

Now I'll fire all the parallel survey calls at once.Now I have everything I need to compile the guide. No `research.json` exists here, so this is standalone Q&A — I'll present the guide without persisting.

---

# Locality Guide: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania — 1793 Yellow Fever Epidemic

## Jurisdiction overview

- **Place:** Philadelphia City and Philadelphia County, Pennsylvania, United States (both established 1682; city and county were coterminous for most practical purposes by 1793)
- **Population during period:** No FamilySearch population data was returned for this fixture; the Wikipedia article on the epidemic records approximately 50,000 residents in 1793, of whom roughly 5,000 died — about one-tenth of the city
- **Philadelphia's role in 1793:** National capital of the United States
- **Epidemic window:** August – November 1793; thousands fled to the surrounding countryside, making absences from the city a documented phenomenon in their own right
- **Key historical sources named in the FamilySearch Wiki** (*Philadelphia Genealogy* article, "Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793" section): burial records from Christ Church, Gloria Dei (Old Swedes'), and other churches; Mathew Carey's published account listing names of the dead; minutes and reports of the citizens' Committee; and records of Bush Hill hospital
- **Primary manuscript repositories:** Philadelphia City Archives and the Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)

## Boundary changes

Philadelphia County was formed in 1682 and its boundaries were stable through the epidemic period. The city and county were governed separately but occupied the same territory. No boundary changes affect record jurisdiction for 1793 research.

---

## Critical context: No civil vital registration in 1793

Pennsylvania did not establish statewide civil registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1906**. There are no government-issued birth, marriage, or death certificates for 1793. **Church records are the only vital records for this period.** The epidemic-specific lists described below are the closest equivalent to a death register.

---

## Available record types

### 🔴 Epidemic-specific records — highest priority for 1793 research

These sources are unique to the outbreak and do not appear in standard genealogical searches:

| Source | What it contains | Where held | Access |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathew Carey, *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever*** (1793) | Appendix lists names of the dead by neighborhood | Digitized; Library Company of Philadelphia; HSP | Published; some digitized editions freely available online |
| **Citizens' Committee minutes and reports** | Relief distribution lists, names of the sick, names of those who fled | Philadelphia City Archives; HSP | Physical/manuscript; some published transcriptions |
| **Bush Hill Hospital records** | Admissions and deaths at the epidemic hospital set up on the city's outskirts | Philadelphia City Archives; HSP | Physical only |
| **Dr. Benjamin Rush's accounts and correspondence** | Clinical notes, patient lists, observations | HSP; Library Company of Philadelphia | Physical manuscript; some published |

> **Research tip (from the FamilySearch Wiki):** The Carey appendix and Committee minutes are the two most genealogically actionable epidemic-specific sources. If your ancestor died in Philadelphia in fall 1793, check these before anything else.

---

### Church records — primary vital source for the period

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (collection ID 1401638) — **1,245,830 records; 607,220 images** — date range covers 1793 — **indexed + images** — searchable at [familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1401638)

The FamilySearch Wiki (*Philadelphia Genealogy*, "Church Records" section) notes:
- Philadelphia's **diverse religious communities** each maintained baptism, marriage, and burial registers
- Many colonial and early national registers have been **transcribed and published**
- The **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** compiled indexes to many Philadelphia church records

Key Philadelphia congregations whose burial registers document epidemic deaths:

| Congregation | Denomination | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Christ Church | Anglican/Episcopal | One of the largest; burial register survives; some digitized |
| Gloria Dei (Old Swedes') | Lutheran/Episcopal | Oldest church in Pennsylvania; registers survive |
| St. Peter's | Episcopal | Sister parish to Christ Church; registers survive |
| St. Mary's | Roman Catholic | Primary Catholic congregation; registers survive |
| First Presbyterian | Presbyterian | Registers survive |
| Mikveh Israel | Jewish | Burial society (chevra kadisha) records |
| African Methodist (Mother Bethel, founded 1794) | AME | Note: Richard Allen and Absalom Jones organized African American relief during the epidemic; their records may document deaths |
| German Lutheran and Reformed churches | Lutheran/Reformed | German-language registers; note language |

**Access:** Many registers are held by the congregations, diocesan archives, or HSP. FamilySearch's *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records* collection includes some Philadelphia registers but coverage is uneven — always check the congregation's own archives and HSP in addition to FamilySearch.

---

### Probate records — surge of filings in late 1793

The epidemic produced an enormous wave of estate filings in Philadelphia County. Probate records name heirs, legatees, creditors, and sometimes neighbors — invaluable for reconstructing families.

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994* (collection ID 1417683) — **893,214 records; 446,607 images** — date range covers 1793 — **indexed + images** — [familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1417683)

Probate files from fall 1793 onward will reflect epidemic mortality. The original registers are held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and the **Register of Wills, Philadelphia**.

---

### Census records

| Census | Coverage | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **1790 federal census** | Heads of household only; taken just before the epidemic — establishes who was in Philadelphia | Indexed + images; FamilySearch and Ancestry |
| **1800 federal census** | Seven years after the epidemic — compare to 1790 to identify households that disappeared | Indexed + images; FamilySearch and Ancestry |

**Strategy:** Cross-referencing 1790 and 1800 census entries against epidemic death lists can confirm or suggest epidemic mortality for a household that vanished between enumerations.

---

### Marriage records

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Civil Marriages, 1677–1950* (collection ID 1571957) — **312,045 records; 156,023 images** — indexed + images — [familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1571957)

**FamilySearch collection:** *Pennsylvania, Compiled Marriages, 1700–1900* (collection ID 1502909) — **198,765 records; 0 images** — index only, no images — [familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909](https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1502909)

Both cover 1793 and may help establish family connections for epidemic survivors or victims.

---

### Land and tax records

**Value:** Philadelphia's city and county tax records list property holders and can document who was assessed before the epidemic vs. who disappears from the rolls afterward — a substitute indicator of death or flight.

- **Philadelphia County deed books** (metes-and-bounds system): held at the **Philadelphia City Archives** and **Philadelphia Recorder of Deeds**; some volumes browsable on FamilySearch
- **County tax lists**: held at the Philadelphia City Archives; help establish residence before and after the epidemic

---

### Newspaper records

Philadelphia had several active newspapers in 1793 publishing death notices, lists of the sick, and Committee announcements:

| Newspaper | Notes |
|---|---|
| *Dunlap's American Daily Advertiser* | Philadelphia's daily; published epidemic death lists |
| *Federal Gazette* | Active during epidemic |
| *General Advertiser* / *Aurora* | Benjamin Franklin Bache's paper |

**Access:** Historical Philadelphia newspapers from this period are available on **Newspapers.com** (subscription) and partially through the **Library of Congress Chronicling America** project (free). The **Library Company of Philadelphia** and **HSP** hold original runs on microfilm.

> **Tip:** Death notices in these papers are often the only dated record of an individual's death before death certificates existed.

---

### Military records

Veterans of the Revolutionary War (ended 1783) living in Philadelphia in 1793 would be in their 30s–60s. Useful records:

- **Revolutionary War pension files** (NARA): name widow and heirs; if a veteran died in the epidemic, a widow's pension claim may document the death
- **Fold3** (subscription): indexes Revolutionary War service and pension records
- **NARA RG 15** (pension files): physical + partially digitized

---

### Immigration records

Philadelphia was the premier American port of entry throughout the 18th century. For ancestors who arrived before or shortly after the epidemic:

- **Philadelphia passenger lists**: NARA microfilm; partially indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch
- **Pennsylvania Archives** (published series): includes some passenger and naturalization lists

---

## Repository guide

### Online repositories

| Repository | Relevant collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | *PA Church and Town Records* (1401638); *PA Probate Records* (1417683); *PA Civil Marriages* (1571957); *PA Compiled Marriages* (1502909); 1790 and 1800 federal census | Free |
| **Ancestry.com** | 1790 and 1800 census (indexed + images); Philadelphia passenger lists; DAR transcriptions | Subscription |
| **Newspapers.com** | Historical Philadelphia newspapers, 1793 | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War pension files; military service records | Subscription |
| **NARA / Fold3** | Pension files, passenger lists | NARA free for some; Fold3 subscription |
| **Library of Congress Chronicling America** | Some early Philadelphia newspapers | Free |

### Physical repositories

| Repository | Key holdings for 1793 research | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Philadelphia City Archives** | Committee minutes, Bush Hill hospital records, probate registers, tax records, deed books | In-person; some records by appointment |
| **Historical Society of Pennsylvania (HSP)** | Manuscript collections including Rush correspondence, Committee records, church registers, Carey materials | In-person; some digitized |
| **Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania (GSP)** | Compiled indexes to Philadelphia church records; published transcriptions | In-person; publications available through interlibrary loan |
| **Library Company of Philadelphia** | Original and early printed editions of Carey's *Short Account*; newspapers | In-person |
| **College of Physicians of Philadelphia** | Medical records, Rush papers, epidemic-era case notes | In-person by appointment |
| **Individual church archives** | Original congregation registers | Contact each congregation or its diocesan archive |
| **Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg)** | Some colonial and early state records | In-person; online catalog |

---

## Records NOT online

The following records are known or likely to exist but are **not fully available in any online database**:

- **Bush Hill Hospital admission and death records** — physical only at Philadelphia City Archives / HSP
- **Citizens' Committee minutes and relief lists** — physical at Philadelphia City Archives / HSP; some transcriptions published but not comprehensively digitized
- **Most individual congregation burial registers** — many remain with churches or at HSP; FamilySearch's coverage in the *Church and Town Records* collection is partial
- **Philadelphia County tax lists, 1790s** — primarily physical at Philadelphia City Archives
- **Philadelphia deed books** — partially browsable on FamilySearch but not name-indexed for this period

---

## Known record losses

No major courthouse fire or catastrophic record loss for Philadelphia has been identified for this period. Philadelphia's records are generally well-preserved. However:

- **African American epidemic victims** are underrepresented in formal church registers; Richard Allen's and Absalom Jones's published account (*A Narrative of the Proceedings of the Black People, During the Late Awful Calamity in Philadelphia*, 1794) is the primary source for the Black community's experience and includes some names
- **Quaker burial records** are held by the **Friends Historical Library at Swarthmore College** and the **Haverford College Quaker Collection**, not at HSP or the City Archives — a frequently missed repository

---

## Research tips

1. **Start with the Carey appendix.** Mathew Carey's *A Short Account of the Malignant Fever* (1793) includes the most widely cited list of epidemic dead. Digitized editions circulate online; HSP and the Library Company hold originals.

2. **Church burial registers are your death certificates.** Since Pennsylvania had no civil registration in 1793, a burial entry in a church register is functionally the death record. Search the FamilySearch *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records* collection first, then contact HSP and the congregation directly for registers not yet digitized.

3. **Probate filings surge in late 1793.** An ancestor who died in the epidemic likely has an estate filing at Philadelphia County. The FamilySearch *Pennsylvania, Probate Records* collection (indexed + images) is the fastest entry point.

4. **Compare 1790 and 1800 censuses.** A household present in 1790 but absent or reduced in 1800 may reflect epidemic mortality. Note that the 1800 census lists heads of household only (as does 1790), so you'll need church and probate records to identify individuals within the household.

5. **Don't overlook refugees.** Thousands fled Philadelphia during August–November 1793. Records of where they went (Germantown, Bristol, Burlington, NJ) may appear in church registers or tax lists of surrounding communities — not in Philadelphia city records.

6. **African American families:** The Allen/Jones *Narrative* (1794) and records of the Free African Society are the primary sources. Mother Bethel AME Church (founded 1794, directly from the epidemic experience) may hold relevant early records.

7. **The Genealogical Society of Pennsylvania** has published extensive transcriptions and indexes of Philadelphia church records. Their *Pennsylvania Genealogical Magazine* and compiled indexes are essential finding aids available through interlibrary loan and at HSP.

8. **Contact HSP directly.** The Historical Society of Pennsylvania holds the richest single concentration of manuscript sources for this event and has experienced staff who can direct you to specific collections.

The Wikipedia article confirms the critical genealogical challenge: the Great Chicago Fire destroyed the Cook County Courthouse, taking with it deeds, birth/death registers, court records, and tax rolls. Now I'll run the full parallel survey.Good data — now let me read the Cook County wiki article found in the search results.I now have all I need. The wiki_place_page calls returned a generic fallback (no Illinois-specific page resolved in this environment); the authoritative Chicago-specific guidance came from `wiki_search` (Cook County, Illinois Genealogy article) and Wikipedia. Here is the full guide.

---

## Locality Research Guide
### Chicago / Cook County, Illinois — Great Fire of 1871
*Place:* Chicago, Cook County, Illinois, United States
*Research window:* c. 1850–1900 (pre-fire, fire year, and reconstruction era)

---

### ⚠️ Critical Context: The Record Destruction

The **Great Chicago Fire (October 8–10, 1871)** destroyed the **Cook County Courthouse**, which housed the city's primary civil records: deeds, birth and death registers, court files, tax rolls, and probate files. An estimated 3.3 square miles burned. Many church registers in the fire zone were also lost. This is the defining constraint of any Chicago genealogy research before 1871 — you cannot assume a record class existed simply because Illinois law required it. The guide below separates what was destroyed, what survived, and how to bridge the gap.

---

### Part 1 — What Was Destroyed

| Record type | Status |
|---|---|
| Pre-1871 Cook County deeds & property records | **Largely destroyed** |
| Pre-1871 birth & death registers | **Destroyed** |
| Pre-1871 court records (probate, naturalizations, civil suits) | **Largely destroyed** |
| Pre-1871 tax rolls | **Destroyed** |
| Church registers in the fire zone | **Many destroyed; some survived** |

---

### Part 2 — What Survived (Pre-Fire Sources)

These are your primary anchors for placing a family in Chicago before 1871.

#### Federal Census Schedules (1850, 1860, 1870)
- The **1850, 1860, and 1870 federal census** schedules for Chicago/Cook County **survived** — originals were held in Washington, D.C., not the courthouse.
- The **1870 census** (taken just before the fire) is especially critical: it can document a household months before the fire struck.
- Also check **mortality schedules** (1850–1880), which name individuals who died in the year preceding each census and may record fire-related deaths.
- **Access:** Indexed on [Ancestry.com](https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/census/) and FamilySearch; also via [NARA](https://www.archives.gov/).

#### City Directories (1840s onward)
- Chicago city directories from the 1840s forward **survived the fire** and are held at the **Newberry Library**.
- Directories list heads of household by name, address, and occupation — a powerful substitute for missing vital records and census years.
- **Access:** Newberry Library (Chicago); some volumes digitized and available through the Internet Archive and Ancestry.

#### Illinois State Census (1855, 1865)
- Illinois conducted state censuses in 1855 and 1865 that were kept at the state level in Springfield — **not in the Cook County Courthouse** — and therefore largely survived.
- **Access:** Illinois State Archives (Springfield); FamilySearch has microfilm of these schedules.

#### Pre-Fire Newspapers
- Chicago newspapers (e.g., the *Chicago Tribune*, founded 1847) reported marriages, deaths, legal notices, and property transfers. Newspaper offices outside the burn zone survived.
- **Access:** [Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/); Newberry Library; Chicago History Museum.

---

### Part 3 — Fire-Era Substitute Sources (Reconstructed Records)

These were created after the fire specifically to replace destroyed records, and are the most direct genealogical bridge across 1871.

#### Burnt Record Act Reconstructed Deeds (1872)
- Illinois passed the **Burnt Record Act of 1872**, requiring property owners to re-file their deeds to re-establish ownership after courthouse destruction.
- FamilySearch holds **1 digitized volume (image group 009982111)** covering post-fire property re-recordings, **1871–1880**, with **112 images** and **41% name-indexed** — meaning you can search part of it by name on FamilySearch and must browse the remainder image-by-image.
- **Access:** FamilySearch (partially searchable via record_search; remainder browse-only).

#### Reconstructed Pre-Fire Court Records
- Some pre-fire court records were reconstructed from copies or surviving fragments.
- FamilySearch holds **1 digitized volume (image group 009982110)** of reconstructed pre-fire Cook County court records, **1865–1871**, with **38 images** — **0% name-indexed and not full-text searchable**. Must be browsed image by image.
- **Access:** FamilySearch browse-only.

#### Chicago Title and Trust Company Records
- Insurance company and title company records became critical substitutes for destroyed property records. The **Chicago Title and Trust Company** collection at the **Chicago History Museum** is particularly important for tracing pre-fire property ownership.
- **Access:** Chicago History Museum (in-person research; some finding aids online).

---

### Part 4 — Post-Fire Records (1871 Onward)

Cook County resumed systematic civil registration almost immediately after the fire. These are indexed and accessible.

| Collection | Records | Date Range | FamilySearch |
|---|---|---|---|
| Illinois, Cook County, Birth Certificates | ~1,450,000 | 1871–1940 | ✅ Indexed |
| Illinois, Cook County, Death Records | ~980,000 | 1871–1947 | ✅ Indexed |
| Illinois, Cook County, Marriage Records | ~620,000 | 1871–1920 | ✅ Indexed |
| Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records | ~285,000 | 1833–1925 | ✅ Indexed |

> **Note on start dates:** The birth, death, and marriage collections all begin at **1871** — reflecting the new post-fire registration system, not civil registration starting that year. Pre-fire vital events are not captured here; use church records and census substitutes for earlier dates.

---

### Part 5 — Church Records

Church registers are the single most important substitute for pre-1871 vital records in Chicago. Parishes outside the burn zone retained their registers; others lost them entirely.

- **FamilySearch** has 285,000 indexed records in the **Illinois, Cook County, Chicago, Church Records, 1833–1925** collection — this covers multiple denominations and parishes.
- **Archdiocese of Chicago — Joseph Cardinal Bernardin Archives:** Holds Catholic parish registers for the Archdiocese. Catholic churches were among Chicago's largest immigrant communities (Irish, German, Polish, Italian). Many registers were held at individual parishes and survived if the church itself survived.
- **Strategy:** Identify the parish by the ancestor's neighborhood and ethnicity, then check whether that specific parish register survived.
- **Access:** Bernardin Archives (Chicago); FamilySearch microfilm; denominational repositories.

---

### Part 6 — Additional Record Types

#### Military Records (Civil War era)
- Chicago was a major Civil War recruitment and mustering center. **Service records** and **pension files** for Illinois soldiers are at NARA and on [Fold3](https://www.fold3.com/). Pension files often contain birth dates, marriage affidavits, and widow's claims — rich substitute sources if a pre-fire vital record is missing.
- Illinois has its own adjutant general records at the Illinois State Archives.

#### Immigration & Naturalization
- Chicago was a major immigrant destination in the 1850s–1880s. Pre-1906 naturalizations could be filed in **any court of record** — after the courthouse fire, re-filings occurred in federal district or state courts in Chicago. Post-1906 naturalizations are at NARA–Chicago.
- **Passenger lists** for arrivals are at NARA; indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch.

#### Cemetery Records
- Cemeteries outside the city held physical interment records unaffected by the fire.
- [FindAGrave](https://www.findagrave.com/) indexes many Chicago-area burials.
- Major historic cemeteries (Graceland, Rosehill, St. Boniface) have their own records.

#### Federal Land / Homestead Records
- Illinois is a federal land state; pre-settlement land patents are at the **Bureau of Land Management General Land Office** (glorecords.blm.gov), held in Washington and unaffected by the fire.

---

### Part 7 — Key Repositories

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Newberry Library** (Chicago) | City directories, maps, manuscripts, genealogical reference | In-person; some digital |
| **Chicago History Museum** | Insurance/title records, photographs, personal papers, newspapers | In-person; some finding aids online |
| **Illinois Regional Archives Depository (IRAD), Northeastern Illinois University** | Cook County records post-1871 | In-person |
| **Archdiocese of Chicago — Bernardin Archives** | Catholic parish registers | In-person by appointment |
| **Cook County Clerk's Office** | Vital records from 1871 onward | In-person; some records via Ancestry |
| **Illinois State Archives (Springfield)** | State census (1855, 1865), statewide records | In-person; some digital |
| **NARA — Great Lakes Region (Chicago)** | Federal court, naturalization, land, military records | In-person |
| **FamilySearch** | Microfilm of post-fire Cook County records; indexed collections above | Online (free) |
| **Ancestry.com** | Federal census, city directories, newspapers | Subscription |

---

### Digitization Level Summary

| Record / Collection | Digitization Status |
|---|---|
| Federal census 1850–1880 | ✅ Indexed + images (Ancestry, FamilySearch) |
| Cook County Birth Certificates 1871–1940 | ✅ Indexed + images (FamilySearch) |
| Cook County Death Records 1871–1947 | ✅ Indexed + images (FamilySearch) |
| Cook County Marriage Records 1871–1920 | ✅ Indexed + images (FamilySearch) |
| Cook County Church Records 1833–1925 | ✅ Indexed + images (FamilySearch) |
| Post-fire Property Re-recordings 1871–1880 | ⚠️ Partially indexed (41%) — remainder browse-only |
| Reconstructed Pre-fire Court Records 1865–1871 | 🔍 Browse-only images — no name index, no full-text search |
| City directories | ⚠️ Partial — some digitized; Newberry holds physical copies |
| Chicago Title & Trust records | 🏛️ Physical only — Chicago History Museum |
| Catholic parish registers (Bernardin Archives) | 🏛️ Largely physical — in-person at the Archives |
| Illinois State Census 1855, 1865 | ⚠️ FamilySearch microfilm; partial digitization |

---

### Research Strategy for Fire-Affected Families

1. **Start with the 1870 federal census** — it captures the household months before the fire and is your most reliable pre-fire anchor.
2. **Work the 1860 and 1850 censuses** backward to establish earlier residence and place of origin.
3. **Check post-fire vital records immediately** — birth, death, and marriage registration resumed in 1871 and is well-indexed.
4. **For property ownership**, search the partially-indexed **Burnt Record Act re-recordings** on FamilySearch, then the Chicago Title & Trust records at the Chicago History Museum.
5. **Identify the ancestor's parish** by neighborhood and ethnicity; check the Bernardin Archives and FamilySearch's Cook County Church Records collection.
6. **Search city directories** at the Newberry Library to bracket residence before and after the fire — many displaced families moved within the city or to suburbs.
7. **Check newspapers** (Newspapers.com, Chicago History Museum) for death notices, legal notices, and fire-relief lists published in October–December 1871.
8. **If the ancestor was a Civil War veteran**, pursue pension files at NARA/Fold3 — these often contain rich pre-fire biographical data.

> **Key quirk:** The Cook County Courthouse destruction means pre-1871 deeds, probate records, court files, and tax rolls are largely gone from the county record system. Records held *outside* Cook County — at the state level in Springfield, at federal repositories in Washington, at church archives — are your primary pre-fire evidence base. Always search outside the county first for pre-1871 facts.

*(Sources: FamilySearch Wiki, "Cook County, Illinois Genealogy" — [Record Loss and the Great Fire](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois_Genealogy#Great_Fire) and [Repositories](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Cook_County,_Illinois_Genealogy#Repositories); Wikipedia, "Great Chicago Fire"; FamilySearch collections and volume search results.)*

output.text_response:
Now I have enough data to compile the guide. The wiki place pages returned only generic fallback content (not Louisiana-specific), but the wiki search and collections search returned solid, actionable data. Here is the structured guide:

---

## 📜 Locality Research Guide: French Colonial Louisiana (c. 1699–1769)

### Jurisdictional Context

**French Louisiana (La Louisiane)** was an administrative district of New France from 1682 to 1762. France founded the colony via René-Robert Cavelier (La Salle) and established New Orleans in 1718. The colony was governed by the **Superior Council (Conseil Supérieur)** and the Catholic Church was the sole legally recognized religious institution — both are critical facts for understanding where records were made and who created them.

In 1762, France secretly ceded Louisiana west of the Mississippi to Spain via the **Treaty of Fontainebleau**. Spanish administration effectively began around **1769**. If your ancestor lived into the 1770s–1800s, their records will cross from French into Spanish colonial record systems — you must search both. The two colonial traditions are one continuous research trail, not separate inquiries.

> **Key boundary note:** Records created before 1762 are French colonial; after ~1769 they are Spanish colonial. The transition years (1762–1769) are administratively thin — Spanish governors had not yet fully replaced French officials, so some French-era record-keeping continued into this overlap.

---

### 1. 📖 Catholic Parish Registers — **HIGHEST PRIORITY**

**What they are:** Baptism, marriage, and burial registers maintained by Catholic priests — the only vital records that existed in French Louisiana.

**Why they matter:** The **Code Noir** (Black Code) required the Catholic baptism of all enslaved persons, so these registers document not only free French colonists and soldiers, but also enslaved Africans, free people of color, and Native American converts. No other record type comes close for family reconstruction in this period.

**Coverage:** St. Louis Cathedral in New Orleans has registers beginning **1720**. Other parishes established later in the colonial period kept their own registers.

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925** (Collection ID: 1612905) — ~987,654 records, ~493,827 images
  🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/1612905

> ⚠️ **Language warning:** Records are in **French** (and often Latin for sacramental formulas). You must be able to read 18th-century French script to use the original images.

**Physical repository:** Louisiana Historical Center, Old U.S. Mint building, New Orleans.

---

### 2. 🏛️ Superior Council Records (Conseil Supérieur)

**What they are:** The governing and judicial body of French Louisiana produced records including:
- **Land grants (concessions)**
- **Court proceedings** (civil and criminal)
- **Contracts and notarial acts**
- **Estate inventories and successions**

These records function like a combined court, land office, and notary for the entire colony — an essential source for property, family relationships, and migration.

**Where held:** Louisiana Historical Center (Old U.S. Mint building, New Orleans) — partially digitized.

**France-side holdings:** The **Archives Nationales d'Outre-Mer (ANOM)** in Aix-en-Provence, France holds administrative correspondence and census rolls for the colony. This is the repository for the metropolitan (French government) side of colonial administration and is not duplicated domestically.
🔗 https://anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr/

> **FamilySearch note:** No dedicated Superior Council collection appears in FamilySearch's Louisiana holdings for this period — these records have not been fully digitized online. Expect to work with microfilm or physical access.

---

### 3. ✍️ New Orleans Notarial Archives

**What they are:** Contracts, wills, marriage settlements, property transactions, and other private legal acts recorded before a notary. The New Orleans Notarial Archives holds the **oldest continuous notarial office in North America**, with records going back to the **1730s**.

**Why they matter:** In French (and later Spanish) legal tradition, nearly every significant private transaction — including marriage contracts, sales, donations, and succession agreements — was executed before a notary. These records often name parents, siblings, and witnesses, making them invaluable for family reconstruction.

**Repository:** New Orleans Notarial Archives Research Center (publicly accessible)
🔗 https://www.notarialarchives.org/

> ⚠️ **Browse-only / physical access:** Not fully available online. Researchers should contact the archives directly or plan an in-person visit.

---

### 4. 📋 Probate / Estate Records

**FamilySearch collection:**
- **Louisiana, Probate Records, 1756–1984** (Collection ID: 2078341) — ~456,789 records, ~228,394 images
  🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/search/collection/2078341

This collection's date range begins in **1756**, overlapping with the final decade of French colonial rule — making it directly relevant for late French-period families. Estate inventories from this era often list household contents, enslaved persons by name, and debts owed to or by the deceased.

> ⚠️ **Language warning:** Pre-1769 probate records will be in French.

---

### 5. 🌾 Land Records and Concessions

**What they are:** French colonial land grants (concessions) were issued by the Superior Council and colonial governors. These are distinct from English-system land patents.

**Where found:**
- **American State Papers: Public Lands** series — documents French and Spanish land grant claims confirmed or adjudicated after the Louisiana Purchase. Accessible via the Library of Congress and Ancestry.com.
- **Louisiana State Land Office** — holds colonial-era concession records.
- **ANOM (France)** — original French crown land grant correspondence.

> **Research tip (from FamilySearch Wiki):** French concessions follow **French and Spanish legal traditions**, not English common law. Searches expecting English-style deeds will not work here — look for "concession" and "arpent" (the French unit of land measure).

---

### 6. 📊 Colonial Census Rolls

French colonial Louisiana conducted periodic census rolls (recensements) of the settler population, which can include household members, enslaved persons, and livestock. These are held at:
- **ANOM** (Aix-en-Provence, France) — primary repository for colonial census rolls
- **Louisiana Historical Center** — some copies and transcriptions

> **Gap warning:** No indexed census collection for French colonial Louisiana appears in FamilySearch's holdings for the 1700–1769 window. These remain largely physical-access or microfilm resources.

---

### 7. 🌐 External Repositories Summary

| Repository | What It Holds | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch.org** | Church records (1718+), probate (1756+) | Free online, partially indexed |
| **Louisiana Historical Center** (New Orleans) | Superior Council records, parish register copies | In-person / partial digitization |
| **New Orleans Notarial Archives** | Notarial acts from 1730s onward | In-person / research center |
| **ANOM** (Aix-en-Provence, France) | Colonial census rolls, administrative correspondence, land grants | In-person / online catalog at anom.archivesnationales.culture.gouv.fr |
| **Louisiana State Land Office** | Colonial concession records | Contact office |
| **American State Papers** | Post-Purchase adjudication of French & Spanish land claims | Free online (LOC, HathiTrust) |
| **St. Louis Cathedral Archives** (New Orleans) | Original parish registers from 1720 | In-person |

---

### ⚠️ Key Quirks and Warnings

1. **All records are in French (and Latin).** There is no English-language shortcut — 18th-century French paleography skills are essential for original documents.
2. **No civil registration existed.** Catholic parish registers are the only vital records. If a family was not Catholic (rare under French rule, but possible for some soldiers and traders), they will be harder to trace.
3. **Records span two colonial regimes.** An ancestor born in the 1740s may have baptism records under French administration and marriage or death records under Spanish administration. Search both systems as one continuous trail.
4. **The Code Noir means church records document enslaved people.** If you are researching African or African-descended ancestors in colonial Louisiana, baptismal registers are your starting point — enslaved persons were recorded by first name with the enslavers' name often noted.
5. **FamilySearch wiki pages for Louisiana** returned only generic content for all four sections (home, getting_started, online_records, research_tips), suggesting Louisiana-specific wiki pages are not yet published at the state level. Consult the FamilySearch Wiki article **"Louisiana Genealogy"** directly at:
   🔗 https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Louisiana_Genealogy#French_Colonial_Records

---

### Research Starting Point Recommendation

Begin with **FamilySearch's Louisiana, Church Records, 1718–1925** (Collection 1612905) for any family present in or near New Orleans. For families in rural concessions or the upper Mississippi settlements, move quickly to the **Louisiana Historical Center** and **ANOM** — these are the authoritative repositories for the French colonial interior and will not be fully represented online.

Would you like a focused research plan for a specific family or locality within colonial Louisiana?
