# Locality Guide: Pennsylvania Anthracite Coal Region (1860s–1890s)
### Counties: Schuylkill · Luzerne · Carbon · Lackawanna · Northumberland
### Focus: Irish immigrant miners and their families

---

## Jurisdiction Overview

| County | Formed | Parent Jurisdiction(s) | County Seat |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Schuylkill** | 1811 | Berks + Northampton | Pottsville |
| **Luzerne** | 1786 | Northumberland | Wilkes-Barre |
| **Carbon** | 1843 | Northampton + Monroe | Jim Thorpe (Mauch Chunk) |
| **Lackawanna** | **1878** | Luzerne | Scranton |
| **Northumberland** | 1772 | Lancaster + Bedford + Cumberland | Sunbury |

- **Population (Schuylkill as bellwether):** 89,510 (1860) → 116,428 (1870) → 129,974 (1880) → 172,927 (1900). The FamilySearch population notes confirm: *"Rapid growth driven by anthracite coal mining. Large immigrant population (Irish, Welsh, German, later Eastern European) from 1840s onward."*
- **Economy:** Anthracite coal extraction, dominated by the Philadelphia & Reading Coal & Iron Company, Delaware, Lackawanna & Western Railroad, and other corporate operators. Work roles for Irish immigrants included collier, miner, laborer, loader, breaker boy, and mule driver.
- **Ethnic composition:** Irish (Famine-era arrivals 1845–1855 and later chain migration), Welsh miners, Germans, with Eastern European influx from the 1880s. Irish settlers were concentrated in Schuylkill (Pottsville, Shenandoah, Mahanoy City), Luzerne/Lackawanna (Scranton, Wilkes-Barre, Pittston), and Carbon (Lansford, Coaldale).
- **Religious denominations:** Roman Catholic (dominant for Irish, served by Dioceses of Harrisburg and Scranton), Methodist and Presbyterian (Welsh), Episcopal, German Lutheran and Reformed.
- **Key historical events:** Molly Maguires trials (Schuylkill, 1875–1877); Long Strike of 1875; repeated mine disasters generating state inspection records; Ancient Order of Hibernians active throughout the region.

---

## ⚠️ Critical Boundary Change

**Lackawanna County did not exist until 1878.** The Scranton/Carbondale/Dunmore area was part of **Luzerne County** until that date. For any event before 1878 in what is now Lackawanna County, search **Luzerne County** records first. The two counties form one continuous research trail for families residing in the northern anthracite field.

Similarly, the **Diocese of Scranton** was not erected until 1868. Catholic parish records from the region before 1868 fall under the **Diocese of Harrisburg**.

---

## Available Record Types

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)
- **Start date:** Pennsylvania did **not** require statewide registration of births, marriages, or deaths until **1906**. Registration in this period was **county-level, inconsistent, and largely unenforced**. Do not expect a civil birth or death certificate for most events 1860–1900.
- **What exists on FamilySearch:** A Pennsylvania-level Death Records volume (1852–1968, 674 images) is digitized at the state level — **browse-only** (0% name-indexed, not full-text searchable). It covers the pre-1906 period but must be browsed image by image.
- **Access:** FamilySearch volume 008120442 — browse only.
- **Substitute:** Church baptismal, marriage, and burial registers are the **primary vital-event source** for this entire period. See Church Records below.

### Church Records
This is the single most important record type for Irish Catholic miners in the 1860s–1890s.

- **Denominations present:** Roman Catholic (most Irish families), Methodist Episcopal, Presbyterian, Episcopal (Anglican for some Anglo-Irish), German Lutheran and Reformed.
- **Catholic parish founding:** Many coal-region Catholic parishes were established between **1840 and 1870** specifically to serve Irish and Eastern European immigrants. Per the *Pennsylvania Coal Region Records* wiki article, baptismal registers for these parishes are held by:
  - **Diocese of Scranton** (1868–present) for northern anthracite counties (Lackawanna, Luzerne, Carbon)
  - **Diocese of Harrisburg** (pre-1868) for southern anthracite counties (Schuylkill, Northumberland) and all coal-region parishes before the Scranton diocese was carved out
- **FamilySearch digitized volumes:** Schuylkill County Church Records 1842–1895 (volume 007936751, 187 images, **browse-only**, English and German — note the German-language entries for non-Irish congregations). This volume must be browsed image by image.
- **FamilySearch indexed collection:** *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records, 1708–1985* (collection 1401638) covers all PA counties and contains 1,245,830 records with 607,220 images. This is **indexed + images** and is the best starting point.
- **Physical repositories:** Diocesan archives in Scranton (570-207-2213) and Harrisburg. Local parishes may retain original registers; contact the parish office directly.
- **Language:** English for Catholic registers; some German Lutheran records require reading the original language.

### Census Records
Census records are the backbone of coal-region research and are largely **indexed and accessible** on FamilySearch and Ancestry.

| Census Year | Notes |
|---|---|
| **1860** | Lists all household members, birthplace, occupation. First coal-boom census — good for Irish Famine-era arrivals. |
| **1870** | Shows birthplace of both individual and parents. Valuable for identifying Irish-born parents of U.S.-born children. |
| **1880** | **First census to state each person's relationship to the household head.** Also records birthplace of parents. Most useful for family reconstruction. Fully indexed. |
| **1890** | Population schedules almost entirely destroyed by 1921 fire. A critical gap. Use 1880 + 1900 + state tax assessments to bridge it. |
| **1900** | Records month/year of birth, years married, number of children born vs. living, year of immigration for foreign-born. Vital for Irish immigrants. |

Per the *Pennsylvania Genealogy* wiki: *"Pennsylvania is covered in all federal censuses 1790–1940; the 1890 population schedules are mostly destroyed. The 1880 census is the first to record the relationship of each household member to the head of household."*

- **State census:** Pennsylvania did not conduct its own separate state census during this period.
- **Mortality schedules (1860, 1870, 1880):** List deaths occurring in the twelve months preceding the census, with cause of death. Valuable for coal-region mine-accident deaths.

### Probate and Court Records
- **Schuylkill County Probate Records 1851–1930:** FamilySearch volume 007936749, 412 images, **browse-only** (0% indexed, not full-text searchable). Browse image by image.
- **Pennsylvania Probate Records 1683–1994:** FamilySearch state-level volume 008120441, 1,180 images, **browse-only**. Also indexed collection 1417683 (*Pennsylvania, Probate Records, 1683–1994*) with 2,304,118 records/images — check both.
- **Naturalizations (critical for Irish immigrants):** Per the *Pennsylvania Naturalization Records* wiki: pre-1906 declarations of intent and petitions were filed in **county courts** — Quarter Sessions, Common Pleas, and Orphans' Courts all had concurrent jurisdiction. These records name country of origin, which directly identifies Irish ancestry.
  - Schuylkill County Naturalization Records 1849–1915: FamilySearch volume 007936750, 298 images, **browse-only** (0% indexed).
  - Pennsylvania Naturalization Records 1790–1915: FamilySearch state-level volume 008120443, 351 images, **15% indexed** (partially indexed + images).
  - After September 1906: naturalization centralized in federal courts — **NARA Philadelphia** (Mid-Atlantic Region) holds post-1906 records for eastern Pennsylvania.

### Land and Deed Records
- **Schuylkill County Deed Records 1815–1900:** FamilySearch volume 007936752, 543 images, **22% name-indexed** (partially indexed + images). Best-digitized Schuylkill volume. Deeds can document Irish miners purchasing homes as they established themselves.
- **System:** Pennsylvania is a metes-and-bounds state. Company-owned mining patches mean many miners rented from coal companies and may not appear in deed records — but boarding-house landlords, saloon keepers, and merchants of Irish origin often do.
- **Access:** County Recorder of Deeds offices for physical originals.

### Marriage Records
- **Schuylkill County Marriage Records 1870–1910:** FamilySearch volume 007936753, 94 images, **browse-only** (0% indexed).
- **Statewide:** Marriage licenses were issued by county courts. The *Pennsylvania, Church and Town Records* collection (1401638) captures some church marriages.
- **Gap warning:** For 1860–1870 marriages in Schuylkill, no indexed FamilySearch volume was returned — check church registers and the Church and Town Records collection.

### ⭐ Specialized Coal-Region Indexes *(FamilySearch Wiki: "Pennsylvania Coal Region Records")*

These are the record types most distinctive to the anthracite region, identified specifically in the wiki article read during this survey:

**1. Lackawanna Valley Anthracite Index, 1855–1898**
Names approximately **41,000 colliery workers** across the lower anthracite field, drawn from company payroll books that survived a 1903 records consolidation. Each entry includes:
- Occupation (loader, breaker boy, mule driver, etc.)
- Wage rate
- **Parish of origin where stated** — directly identifies Irish origin parishes

This is a premier finding aid for immigrant miners whose names were indexed inconsistently in census records.

**2. Pennsylvania Triennial State Tax Assessments (from 1853)**
State assessors enumerated coal-region residents every three years from 1853. Ledgers record: householder name, real estate value, occupation, and sometimes nativity. These assessments **directly substitute for the missing 1890 census** and fill other gaps. They are held at the Pennsylvania State Archives.

**3. Mine Inspection and Accident Reports**
The Pennsylvania Bureau of Mine Inspection published annual reports listing fatalities by colliery. These are organized by mine, not by person, but name individual victims. Invaluable for documenting mining deaths not captured in civil records. Held at the Pennsylvania State Archives and many are available through HathiTrust/Google Books.

### Military Records
- **Civil War (1861–1865):** Many Irish immigrants served in Pennsylvania infantry and artillery regiments. Service and pension records at **NARA Washington, D.C.** (pension files are the richest source — they often include marriage certificates, widow depositions, and birth affidavits). Pennsylvania State Archives holds state muster rolls.
- **Draft registrations:** The 1863 Civil War draft registered all male residents 20–45; coal-region records survive at NARA.
- **Veterans schedules (1890):** The veteran-specific schedule of the 1890 census survives even though the main population schedules were destroyed — covers Union veterans and widows.

### Cemetery Records
- **Major coal-region cemeteries:** Many are denominational — Catholic, Methodist, and Welsh Congregationalist cemeteries exist in nearly every patch town.
- **FindAGrave and BillionGraves:** Significant coverage for the larger cemeteries (Annunciation, St. Patrick's, St. Joseph's parishes in Pottsville/Shenandoah, etc.), but rural patch-town cemeteries may have limited or no online transcription.
- **Physical records:** Sexton records held by parishes or cemetery associations. Contact the Diocese of Scranton or Harrisburg for Catholic cemeteries.

### Newspaper Records
- **Key papers:** *Miners' Journal* (Pottsville, est. 1825 — essential for Schuylkill County); *Scranton Tribune*; *Wilkes-Barre Times*; *Carbon Advocate* (Lehighton).
- **Miners' Journal** is the most valuable single newspaper for Irish coal-region genealogy — it published mine-accident notices, Molly Maguires trial coverage, AOH lodge news, and death/marriage notices.
- **Access:** Pennsylvania State Library (Harrisburg), Hagley Museum and Library, and local county historical societies hold microfilm runs. Some issues digitized at Chronicling America (Library of Congress) and Newspapers.com (subscription).
- **Content value:** Obituaries often name county of origin in Ireland, surviving family members, fraternal lodge affiliations, and length of US residence.

### Immigration and Naturalization Records
- **Relevant ports:** Philadelphia (primary), New York/Castle Garden/Ellis Island (1892+), Baltimore.
- **Passenger lists:** Pre-1891 lists at NARA; fully indexed on Ancestry and FamilySearch for most years. The 1860s–1880s lists record name, age, sex, occupation, and country of origin. For Irish immigrants, list the county and townland of origin when possible.
- **Content note:** Examine the full manifest for every family member — infants and young children traveling with parents are listed. In multi-family groups, chain-migration companions from the same Irish townland often appear on adjacent manifest lines.
- **Naturalization:** See Probate and Court Records above. Pre-1906 declarations filed in county courts throughout the region.

### Tax Records
- **Pennsylvania triennial assessments from 1853:** See Specialized Indexes above. Also substitute for the missing 1890 census.
- **Annual county tax duplicates:** Held at county courthouses and the Pennsylvania State Archives. List all taxable residents including boarders with personal property.
- **Access:** Pennsylvania State Archives (Harrisburg); some counties have transferred older records to local historical societies.

### Occupational and Company Records
- **Coal company employment records:** The payroll books underlying the Lackawanna Valley Anthracite Index are the best-known survival. Philadelphia & Reading Coal & Iron Company records are held partly at the Hagley Museum and Library (Wilmington, DE).
- **Mine inspection reports:** Annual Bureau of Mine Inspection reports, Pennsylvania State Archives.
- **Ancient Order of Hibernians (AOH):** The Irish Catholic fraternal organization had lodges in every major coal town. Lodge membership registers and minute books, where extant, are held by individual lodge secretaries or the AOH national archives. Some have been deposited at county historical societies.
- **Molly Maguires trial records (1875–1877):** Schuylkill County Court of Quarter Sessions. These transcripts and related records name hundreds of Irish mining families and are held at the Schuylkill County Historical Society, Pottsville.

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | Key Collections | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | Pennsylvania Church & Town Records 1708–1985 (collection 1401638, 1.24M records); Pennsylvania Probate Records 1683–1994 (collection 1417683, 2.3M images); Schuylkill deed/probate/naturalization/marriage volumes (see above) | Free |
| **Ancestry** | US Federal Censuses (all years, indexed + images); Pennsylvania Tax & Exoneration Lists 1768–1801; 1850 Schuylkill County Census | Subscription |
| **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress) | *Miners' Journal* and other PA coal-region newspapers | Free |
| **HathiTrust / Google Books** | Pennsylvania Bureau of Mine Inspection annual reports | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Key Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Pennsylvania State Archives** (Harrisburg) | Triennial tax assessments; state vital records post-1906; state census substitutes; state military records | In-person / limited mail |
| **NARA Mid-Atlantic Region** (Philadelphia) | Post-1906 naturalization records; federal court records | In-person / digitized portions online |
| **NARA Washington, D.C.** | Civil War service & pension records; draft registrations; pre-1891 passenger lists | In-person / Fold3 (subscription) |
| **Schuylkill County Historical Society** (Pottsville) | Molly Maguires records; local newspapers; county histories; genealogy files | In-person |
| **Lackawanna County Historical Society** (Scranton) | Northern anthracite region records | In-person |
| **Luzerne County Historical Society** (Wilkes-Barre) | Pre-1878 Lackawanna-area records; Wyoming Valley records | In-person |
| **Diocese of Scranton Archives** | Catholic parish registers post-1868 for Lackawanna, Luzerne, Carbon | Contact in advance |
| **Diocese of Harrisburg Archives** | Catholic parish registers pre-1868 for all coal counties; Schuylkill and Northumberland parishes throughout | Contact in advance |
| **Hagley Museum and Library** (Wilmington, DE) | Philadelphia & Reading Coal & Iron Company employment records | In-person / advance appointment |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following exist but have **no digitized online version**:
- Pennsylvania triennial state tax assessments (1853 onward) — Pennsylvania State Archives only
- AOH lodge membership and minute books — individual lodges or county historical societies
- Coal company employment records (except Lackawanna Valley Anthracite Index)
- Schuylkill County Naturalization Records 1849–1915 and Church Records 1842–1895 — FamilySearch has images but they are **browse-only with no name index** (volumes 007936750 and 007936751)
- Schuylkill County Probate Records 1851–1930 and Marriage Records 1870–1910 — same: images present, **browse-only** (volumes 007936749 and 007936753)
- Mine inspection accident reports (physical copies at PA State Archives; some annual reports on HathiTrust)
- Diocesan Catholic parish registers not yet microfilmed or digitized

---

## Known Record Losses

- **1890 Federal Census:** Almost entirely destroyed. Substitute with: 1880 census + 1900 census + Pennsylvania triennial tax assessments + city/borough directories + church records.
- **Pennsylvania civil vital registration (1860–1905):** The state simply did not require it. There are no missing records to find — they were never created for most events. Church registers and county marriage-license records are the primary substitute.
- **Pre-formation records for Lackawanna County:** All records before 1878 are in **Luzerne County** holdings, not Lackawanna's.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the Lackawanna Valley Anthracite Index (1855–1898).** If your ancestor worked in the lower anthracite field, this index of ~41,000 workers often records the parish of origin — a direct bridge back to Irish origins. Source: *Pennsylvania Coal Region Records* (FamilySearch wiki).

2. **The 1880 census is your linchpin.** It's the first to show household relationships and parent birthplaces. Use it to anchor an Irish mining family before working forward to 1900 and backward to 1860/1870.

3. **Lackawanna before 1878 = Luzerne County.** Any event in the Scranton/Carbondale/Dunmore area before Lackawanna County was formed in 1878 is in Luzerne County records. Always search Luzerne first for northern anthracite families.

4. **Diocese of Scranton (post-1868) vs. Diocese of Harrisburg (pre-1868).** Catholic parish registers follow diocesan boundaries, not county lines. Pre-1868 baptisms anywhere in the coal region are in Harrisburg's custody.

5. **Naturalization records name country of origin.** Pre-1906 declarations and petitions are scattered across county Quarter Sessions, Common Pleas, and Orphans' Courts. The Schuylkill County naturalization volume (007936750, 1849–1915, 298 images) must be browsed image by image — there is no name index.

6. **Pennsylvania death records 1852–1968 exist but are browse-only** on FamilySearch (volume 008120442, 674 images, 0% indexed). For deaths before statewide mandatory registration in 1906, cross-reference church burial registers, cemetery records, and newspaper obituaries.

7. **Read the Miners' Journal (Pottsville) obituaries.** Irish coal-region obituaries routinely name the county in Ireland the deceased emigrated from, their lodge affiliations, and surviving family — information rarely captured in official records.

8. **Mine accident mortality schedules.** The census mortality schedules (1860, 1870, 1880) record deaths in the preceding year including mine fatalities. Cross them with Bureau of Mine Inspection annual reports to document cause of death.

9. **The Schuylkill deed volume (22% indexed) is the best-digitized county record.** Use FamilySearch volume 007936752 for land/property transactions 1815–1900. The other Schuylkill volumes (probate, naturalization, church, marriage) are all browse-only.

10. **AOH lodge records for social network.** The Ancient Order of Hibernians organized Irish Catholic miners across the region. Lodge records, where extant at county historical societies, can reveal townland-of-origin clusters and family networks that census records obscure.

---

*Survey conducted 2026-07-23. Place data: FamilySearch place_search (Schuylkill County standardPlace confirmed; Luzerne, Carbon, Lackawanna, Northumberland resolve only to Pennsylvania state level in the FamilySearch place database — county-specific volume searches for those four counties are not separately available via this tool). Collections data: FamilySearch collections_search (Pennsylvania scope). Volume data: FamilySearch volume_search (Schuylkill county-level: 5 volumes; Pennsylvania state-level: 3 volumes). Specialized coal-region indexes: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Coal Region Records" (https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Pennsylvania_Coal_Region_Records). Pennsylvania genealogy guidance: FamilySearch Wiki, "Pennsylvania Genealogy" and "Pennsylvania Naturalization Records."*

# Locality Guide: Vermont (Pre-Statehood, c. 1749–1791)

## Jurisdiction Overview

| | |
|---|---|
| **Status during target period** | Not a U.S. state. Existed successively as the **New Hampshire Grants** (c. 1749–1777) and then the independent **Vermont Republic** (January 1777 – March 4, 1791) |
| **Admitted to the Union** | March 4, 1791 (14th state) |
| **Parent claimants** | New Hampshire (grants issued 1749–1764), then New York (claimed jurisdiction 1764–1777) — both competing; Vermont ultimately rejected both |
| **Primary governing unit** | The **town** (New England township model). Towns are where nearly all records in this period were created and are still held |
| **Economy** | Subsistence and small-scale farming; saw and grist milling; potash production; some river-valley trade |
| **Ethnic composition** | Overwhelmingly English-descent New Englanders moving in from Connecticut, Massachusetts, and New Hampshire |
| **Religious denominations** | Congregational (dominant); Baptist; some Presbyterian, Anglican/Episcopal, and Methodist (Methodist arriving toward the end of the period) |
| **Population** | No data returned by the population tool for this period. Based on historical record, Vermont's population grew rapidly from near-zero European settlement in the 1760s to roughly 85,000 by 1790. |
| **Key events** | New Hampshire Grants dispute (1749–1777); Battle of Bennington (1777); Vermont Republic era (1777–1791); Revolutionary War |

> **FamilySearch Wiki note:** The Vermont Genealogy page ([https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Vermont_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Vermont_Genealogy)) explicitly flags that Vermont's pre-statehood land records must not be confused with post-statehood records, and identifies the 1771 Grant Survey Schedule as the earliest systematic resident list for the region.

---

## Boundary and Jurisdictional History

This is the most critical context for pre-statehood Vermont research:

- **1749–1764:** New Hampshire Governor Benning Wentworth issued grants for over 130 townships in the territory west of the Connecticut River. Grantees received town charters and settled under New Hampshire authority. Land records from this era are in the **New Hampshire State Archives** (Concord, NH).
- **1764:** The British Privy Council assigned the territory to New York. New York refused to honor the NH grants and issued competing patents to different grantees — creating duplicated, conflicting land claims.
- **1777:** Vermont declared itself an independent republic on January 15, 1777, and adopted its own constitution in July 1777. Vermont rejected both New York and New Hampshire authority. The Vermont Republic began issuing its own land grants and organizing its own county and town governments.
- **First Vermont counties (formed 1778):** Bennington, Chittenden, Rutland, Windsor. Additional counties followed through the 1780s (Addison, Caledonia, Essex, Orange, Orleans, Washington, Windham).
- **1791:** Vermont admitted to the Union; existing county and town structure carried forward intact.

> **Practical impact:** A family living in, say, Bennington in 1773 may appear in New York land records (the competing patent system) *and* in New Hampshire grant records *and* in Vermont Republic records — three overlapping documentary trails for the same tract of land.

---

## Available Record Types

### Land Records ⭐ *PRIMARY SOURCE for this period*

Land records are the most important and most abundant record class for pre-statehood Vermont. No civil vital registration existed; land is where people left their names.

**New Hampshire Grants (c. 1749–1764)**
- Township charters granted by NH Governor Benning Wentworth list original proprietors (grantees) by name
- **The Vermont 1771 Grant Survey Schedule** — documented by the FamilySearch Vermont Genealogy wiki page — was prepared under New York colonial authority and enumerates approximately **1,800 grantees** of NH grants that New York refused to recognize. Each entry lists the grantee, township grant date, acreage, and disputed-claim status. This is **the earliest systematic resident list for the future state of Vermont** and predates any civil registration
- Repository: **New Hampshire State Archives** (Concord) and **Vermont State Archives** (Montpelier)

**Town Proprietors' Records (c. 1760s–1790s)**
- Each granted township organized a "proprietors' meeting"; minutes and records name early landowners, lot assignments, and settler disputes
- 19th-century town histories (most published 1860–1900) often reprint these colonial-era proprietors' records in full — noted explicitly by the FamilySearch Vermont wiki as an important access point when originals are difficult to reach
- Held at: individual town clerk offices; some published in town histories available at Vermont historical societies and digitized on Google Books/Hathi Trust

**Vermont Republic Land Grants (1777–1791)**
- The Vermont Republic re-granted disputed lands and issued new patents
- Repository: **Vermont State Archives**, Montpelier

**Deeds**
- Recorded at the town level (Vermont is a state-land state using the New England township system, not the federal rectangular survey)
- Town clerk offices hold deed books from the late 1770s or 1780s onward depending on when each town was organized
- Many Vermont town deed books have been microfilmed by FamilySearch; check the **FamilySearch Catalog** by individual town name
- Digitization level: **varies by town** — some are indexed + images on FamilySearch; others are browse-only microfilm

---

### Vital Records (Civil Registration)

- **Start date for statewide civil registration:** 1857 — confirmed by FamilySearch Vermont wiki
- **For the pre-statehood period:** There is NO statewide civil registration. Individual town clerks recorded some births, marriages, and deaths beginning in the 1770s–1780s, but the FamilySearch wiki explicitly notes this coverage is **uneven** — some towns recorded vital events consistently; many did not
- **Where held:** Town clerk offices for each Vermont town. Many early town vital records have been microfilmed; use the FamilySearch Catalog by town name to find what's available
- **Pre-registration alternatives:** Church records (see below), family bibles, gravestone inscriptions, probate records, and town histories

---

### Church Records

Church registers are the **de facto vital records** for this period.

- **Congregational** churches were present in nearly every settled Vermont town by the 1780s. Records include baptisms, marriages, and burials. Held at individual churches and at the **Congregational Library & Archives** (Boston, MA); some microfilmed by FamilySearch
- **Baptist** churches established in Vermont from the 1770s. Records scattered at individual churches; some held by the **American Baptist Historical Society**
- **Presbyterian** and **Anglican/Episcopal** congregations present in some towns; records at denominational archives
- **Methodist** records begin toward the very end of this period (late 1780s–1790s)
- FamilySearch has microfilmed many Vermont church registers; search the FamilySearch Catalog under the specific town and then "Church records"
- Digitization level: **browse-only microfilm** for most; some indexed collections exist for specific denominations

---

### Census Records

**1790 Federal Census (Vermont)**
- Vermont was admitted March 4, 1791, after the main 1790 census enumeration (begun August 2, 1790). Vermont was enumerated in **1791** as a supplement to the 1790 census. These records are available and typically appear under "1790 Census"
- Lists head of household only; all other household members counted by age/sex categories only
- Digitization level: **Indexed + images** — available on FamilySearch (free) and Ancestry (subscription)
- Repository online: FamilySearch, Ancestry

**Pre-1790 Enumerations**
- Vermont was an independent republic, not a U.S. territory, so there is no pre-1790 federal census or territorial census
- **No Vermont state or republic census** survives from this period
- **Substitute:** Vermont town Grand Lists (property tax lists) — see Tax Records below

---

### Tax Records / Grand Lists ⭐ *Critical census substitute*

Vermont towns maintained **Grand Lists** — annual property tax inventories listing taxable residents, their property, and assessed values. These begin in the late 1770s–early 1780s for most organized towns and are the closest available substitute for a pre-1790 census.

- Can identify residents, approximate wealth, and year-to-year presence/absence in a town
- Held at: individual town clerk offices; some microfilmed by FamilySearch (search the FamilySearch Catalog by town)
- Digitization level: **browse-only microfilm** for most; not systematically indexed

---

### Probate Records

- Vermont's probate courts were organized by county/district from the late 1770s onward as the Republic established its governmental structure
- Records include wills, estate inventories, letters of administration, guardianships, and estate distributions — rich sources for naming family members when vital records are absent
- The earliest probate records date from approximately **1778–1780** for the original four counties
- Held at: county probate district offices; many have been microfilmed by FamilySearch
- Digitization level: **browse-only microfilm** for most; check the FamilySearch Catalog by county

---

### Military Records

Vermont's pre-statehood period coincides directly with the Revolutionary War (1775–1783).

**Revolutionary War Service Records**
- Vermont militia records, including Ethan Allen's Green Mountain Boys and later Vermont Continental troops
- Federal service records are at **NARA** (National Archives, Washington, DC / St. Louis)
- FamilySearch has a collection of Revolutionary War records; Fold3 (subscription, listed in the external links returned by FamilySearch) is a key repository
- Vermont Adjutant General's records also hold some state-level militia rolls

**Pension and Bounty Land Files**
- Veterans (and widows) who applied for pensions after the war filed detailed pension applications at NARA — often the most genealogically rich Revolutionary War records, naming parents, siblings, marriage dates, and witnesses
- Available indexed + images on FamilySearch and Fold3
- Search: Ancestry's *U.S., Revolutionary War Pension and Bounty-Land Warrant Application Files*

**Vermont State Archives**
- Holds Vermont-specific militia rosters and rolls

---

### Probate and Court Records

- County courts were established beginning 1778 in the four original Vermont counties
- Court records include civil suits, criminal cases, and can document family disputes, debt, and property conflicts
- Held at county courthouses and Vermont State Archives
- Digitization level: **microfilm or physical** for most of this period

---

### Cemetery Records

- Gravestone inscriptions often survive where town vital records and church registers do not — critically important for this period
- The **Vermont Old Cemetery Association** (VOCA) has surveyed many pre-1800 burial grounds
- **Find A Grave** ([findagrave.com](https://www.findagrave.com)) — listed in FamilySearch's curated external links — provides online coverage for many Vermont cemeteries, including early gravestone photos
- Physical sexton records for this early period are sparse; gravestone evidence is usually the primary source

---

### Newspaper Records

- Vermont's first newspapers appeared in 1781:
  - *Vermont Gazette* (Bennington, est. 1781)
  - *Vermont Journal* (Windsor, est. 1783)
- Content value: legal notices, land sales, death notices, and public affairs
- Some issues have been digitized and are available through **Newspapers.com** (listed in FamilySearch's curated external links) and **Chronicling America** (Library of Congress, free)
- Coverage before 1791 is fragmentary; do not expect consistent vital-event notices

---

### Immigration / Emigration Records

- Most Vermont settlers arrived overland from Connecticut, Massachusetts, and New Hampshire — no passenger list documentation
- The relevant "migration records" for this population are the NH grant records (documenting when families took up grants) and town proprietors' records
- Post-Revolutionary immigration to Vermont from other states sometimes documented in town proprietors' minutes or deed chains
- Formal passenger list records are not applicable for most pre-statehood Vermont families

---

## Repository Guide

### Online Repositories

| Repository | What's available for pre-statehood Vermont | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **FamilySearch** | 1790/91 federal census (indexed + images); some town vital records; probate microfilm; church record microfilm; Revolutionary War records; deed microfilm — search by individual town in FamilySearch Catalog | Free |
| **Ancestry** | 1790 federal census; Revolutionary War pension files; some Vermont vital record collections | Subscription |
| **Fold3** | Revolutionary War service records, pension files, military rolls | Subscription |
| **Find A Grave** | Cemetery records and gravestone photos for Vermont | Free |
| **Newspapers.com** | Some early Vermont newspaper runs (post-1781) | Subscription |
| **NARA (archives.gov)** | Revolutionary War service records, pension files; original federal census schedules | Free finding aids; some records fee-based |
| **Hathi Trust / Google Books** | 19th-century Vermont town histories (many fully digitized and free), which reprint colonial-era proprietors' records | Free |

### Physical Repositories

| Repository | Holdings | Access |
|---|---|---|
| **Vermont State Archives & Records Administration** (Montpelier, VT) | Vermont Republic land grants; early state records; Vermont Adjutant General military records; some county records | In-person; some online finding aids |
| **New Hampshire State Archives** (Concord, NH) | Original New Hampshire grant records, Benning Wentworth township charters listing original proprietors | In-person; some microfilm through FHL |
| **New York State Archives** (Albany, NY) | New York colonial patents for Vermont territory (the competing land grants) | In-person; some microfilm |
| **Vermont town clerk offices** | Town vital records (from 1770s–1780s, unevenly); deed books; grand lists (tax records) | In-person; contact individual town |
| **Vermont Historical Society** (Barre, VT) | Manuscript collections, town histories, genealogical files, published compilations | In-person; some materials digitized |
| **Family History Library / FamilySearch** (Salt Lake City, UT; or any FHC) | Microfilm of most Vermont town and county record sets | Free (some via FamilySearch Catalog online) |
| **NARA (Washington, DC / St. Louis)** | Revolutionary War service and pension records | In-person or mail request; many digitized |
| **Congregational Library & Archives** (Boston, MA) | Congregational church registers for Vermont churches | In-person; some microfilmed |

---

## Records NOT Online

The following record types exist but are **not fully available in online databases** and require consulting physical repositories or FamilySearch microfilm (browse-only):

- **Town Grand Lists (tax records)** — most are microfilm only; not name-indexed online
- **Town proprietors' meeting minutes** — primarily in town clerk offices and published town histories; not systematically digitized
- **Most pre-1857 town vital records** — microfilmed for some towns but not indexed; must browse or use the FHL catalog to locate the reel
- **Vermont county probate records (1778–1800)** — microfilm available through FHL for most counties but largely browse-only
- **New Hampshire grant records** — held at NH State Archives; some on microfilm but not widely digitized
- **New York colonial patent records** for Vermont territory — NY State Archives; physical access required for most
- **Vermont county court records** — physical at county courthouses; limited microfilm coverage
- **Early church registers** — many denominations' Vermont records have not been digitized; microfilm at FHL for some

---

## Known Record Losses and Gaps

- **No pre-statehood census exists.** Vermont was an independent republic, not a U.S. territory — there is no 1780 or pre-1790 federal or territorial census. Grand Lists (town tax lists) are the only systematic annual enumeration of residents.
- **Pre-1857 vital registration is absent at the state level.** Town-level recording is uneven; expect gaps. For many families, you will find no official birth, marriage, or death record before 1857.
- **Conflicting land claims.** The three-way dispute (NH, NY, Vermont Republic) means some families appear in records for jurisdictions that ultimately had no legal authority over them. You may need to search all three to reconstruct a complete land history.
- **Early church records are scattered.** Not all early Vermont congregations kept registers, and not all that did have had their records preserved or microfilmed.
- **Some town records were lost to courthouse fires and storage neglect** in the 19th century. Where a town's records are sparse, the Vermont Historical Society may have transcriptions or compiled genealogies.

---

## Research Tips

1. **Start with the 1771 Grant Survey Schedule.** As the FamilySearch Vermont wiki explicitly documents, this is the earliest systematic resident list for Vermont — ~1,800 named grantees with township, acreage, and claim status. If your ancestor was an early settler, this is your first stop.

2. **Work the town, not the state.** Vermont's pre-statehood records are decentralized at the town level. Identify the specific town your ancestor lived in, then search that town's deed books, grand lists, vital records, and town history separately. FamilySearch Catalog searches by town name are essential.

3. **Read the 19th-century town history.** As noted in the FamilySearch Vermont wiki, town histories published 1860–1900 routinely reprint colonial-era proprietors' records, early settler lists, and family genealogies not available elsewhere. Many are fully digitized on Google Books and Hathi Trust for free.

4. **Chase the land through three jurisdictions.** For families present before 1777, search: (1) NH Wentworth grant records, (2) NY colonial patents (if the family was caught in the NY-grant zone), and (3) Vermont Republic land grants. These three trails are not redundant — each may name different family members or witnesses.

5. **Grand Lists are your census substitute.** Before 1790, the annual town Grand List is the only way to track a Vermont resident year by year. Absence from a Grand List may indicate death, removal, or poverty (non-taxable); presence confirms residency and gives approximate economic standing.

6. **Revolutionary War pensions are genealogically rich.** If any male ancestor was old enough to serve (born c. 1740–1765), file a pension application search at NARA. Widow's pension applications in particular often give marriage dates, husband's birth information, and witnesses' affidavits naming relatives.

7. **The 1790/1791 census is your first federal snapshot.** Because Vermont entered the Union in March 1791, its census was taken that year. It lists all heads of household and is fully indexed and available free on FamilySearch. It names only the head of household, but the household structure (free males under 16, over 16, females, slaves) provides demographic clues.

8. **Spelling variation is significant.** Early Vermont records were kept by town clerks of varying literacy. Surnames may be spelled phonetically and inconsistently — especially important when searching indexes.

9. **Contact the Vermont Genealogical Society and Vermont Historical Society** for compiled genealogies and manuscript materials not online. Both maintain research libraries with Vermont family files.

---

## External Links (FamilySearch-Curated, returned for Vermont 1760–1791)

FamilySearch returned 6 curated external resources for Vermont in this time window:
- [U.S. Federal Census Collection — Ancestry.com](https://www.ancestry.com/search/collections/census/) *(subscription)*
- [Vital Records — FamilySearch Wiki](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Category:Vital_Records) *(free)*
- [Cemetery Records — Find A Grave](https://www.findagrave.com/) *(free)*
- [Historical Newspapers — Newspapers.com](https://www.newspapers.com/) *(subscription)*
- [Military Records — Fold3](https://www.fold3.com/) *(subscription)*
- [National Archives and Records Administration (NARA)](https://www.archives.gov/) *(free finding aids)*

> **Note on external links:** The returned set is generic (platform-level links) rather than Vermont-collection-specific links. For Vermont-targeted searches on these platforms, navigate to each site and filter to Vermont + the relevant record type.

---

**FamilySearch Wiki pages consulted:**
- *Vermont Genealogy* — [https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Vermont_Genealogy](https://www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Vermont_Genealogy) — all four wiki sections (home, getting_started, online_records, research_tips) resolved to this single page; content cited above is drawn directly from its text.

dimensions.name: Research strategy
dimensions.rationale: The guide provides actionable, specific research strategy: (1) Start with the 1771 Grant Survey Schedule (earliest systematic resident list, ~1,800 grantees); (2) Work the town, not the state, using FamilySearch Catalog by town name; (3) Read 19th-century town histories (1860–1900) which reprint colonial proprietors' records, available on Google Books/Hathi Trust; (4) Chase land through three jurisdictions (NH, NY, Vermont Republic); (5) Use Grand Lists as census substitute; (6) Search Revolutionary War pensions at NARA for genealogically rich details; (7) Use 1790/91 census as first federal snapshot; (8) Account for spelling variation; (9) Contact Vermont Genealogical Society and Vermont Historical Society. Includes specific pitfalls (conflicting land claims, uneven vital registration, record losses to courthouse fires) and specific collection references.
